pages.name: advantages
pages.title: Advantages & Differentiators
pages.content: > **JMDT вҖ” The Truth Layer for Verifiable Information.** JMDT uniquely combines scalability, privacy, and enterprise DAGs with AVC consensus and zkVM-based Ethereum finality.

pages.name: advantages
pages.title: Head-to-Head Comparison
pages.content:
The following table compares **Jupiter Meta Data Token (JMDT)** with leading L2 blockchain infrastructure projects across key dimensions relevant to both enterprise and developer adoption.

| Feature | **JMDT** | Polygon zkEVM | Starknet | Aztec Network | Hyperledger Fabric |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Layer Type** | L2 + L3 (Enterprise DAG) | L2 (zkEVM) | L2 (STARK Rollup) | L2 (Private zk-SNARKs) | Private Consortium |
| **Scalability (TPS)** | 2,000+ (L2); 10K+ (L3 DAG) | 2,000вҖ“4,000 | 3,000+ | <100 (privacy bottleneck) | Depends on config |
| **Privacy Model** | zk-SNARK/STARK + DID + zkVM | zk-SNARKs | zk-STARKs (public) | zk-SNARKs (Private Tx) | No native privacy |
| **ZK Circuit Strategy** | Rust + RISC Zero zkVM | Circom + Groth16 | Cairo VM | Circom + Aztec Noir | None |
| **Finality Time (L2)** | ~3вҖ“10 sec | ~5вҖ“10 min | ~10вҖ“15 min | ~30вҖ“60 min | Instant (centralised) |
| **L1 Settlement** | Dynamic zk-rollup to L1 | Periodic zk-rollup | STARK rollup | Delayed zk-rollup | Not applicable |
| **Data Availability** | zk-anchored + immudb logs | Ethereum DA Layer | On-chain calldata | On-chain | Off-chain (private) |
| **Enterprise Readiness** | вң… DAG layer, DID, privacy | вҡ пёҸ Limited DID/enterprise SDKs | вҡ пёҸ Limited identity & compliance | вқҢ Not enterprise-ready | вң… Proven enterprise use |
| **Token Model** | JMDT (Governance + Utility) | MATIC (Gas & Staking) | STRK | AZTEC | None |
| **Smart Contract Support** | Rust (zkVM) + Solidity | Solidity (zkEVM) | Cairo | Aztec Noir + limited Solidity | Chaincode (Go/Java) |
| **Governance** | Foundation DAO | Off-chain + Foundation | TBD (future DAO) | Aztec Foundation | Centralised consortium |

pages.name: advantages
pages.title: Key Takeaways
pages.content:
**JMDT uniquely bridges the gap** between public zk-rollups (like Starknet) and enterprise-grade blockchains (like Hyperledger):

- **Privacy-first by design** вҖ” ZK-SNARK/STARK proofs, DID-based authentication, and on-device ZK computation ensure no PII is ever exposed on-chain
- **Enterprise DAG architecture** вҖ” L3 DAG nodes provide 10K+ TPS for private, high-throughput business logic, with zkRollup commitments to L2 for global auditability
- **Fastest L2 finality** вҖ” AVC consensus achieves ~3вҖ“10s finality vs. 5вҖ“60 minutes for alternatives
- **RISC Zero zkVM** вҖ” Rust-based circuits are auditable, version-controlled, and quantum-resistant (STARKs), unlike Cairo or Circom approaches
- **Decentralised identity** вҖ” native W3C DID support gives JMDT a clear advantage for GDPR-compliant and audit-friendly use cases вҖ” a gap none of the competitors fill natively
- **Immutable audit trails via immudb** вҖ” append-only, tamper-proof logs for enterprise compliance across all layers

pages.name: advantages
pages.title: Why Developers Choose JMDT
pages.content:
- **Full EVM compatibility** вҖ” deploy existing Solidity contracts without modification
- **Rust-based ZK development** вҖ” write ZK circuits in familiar Rust, compiled to RISC Zero zkVM
- **DAG SDK + APIs** вҖ” build business logic in Python or Rust on L3
- **JMDT.io RPC** вҖ” standard JSON-RPC endpoint compatible with existing Web3 tooling
- **JMDN node** вҖ” run your own node with full participation in AVC consensus

pages.content:
id: architecture
title: Three-Layer Architecture
sidebar_label: Architecture
description: JMDT's three-layer architecture вҖ” L3 Enterprise DAG, L2 ZK-Rollup + AVC consensus, and L1 Ethereum settlement. Learn how each layer ensures scalability, security, and privacy.
keywords: [JMDT architecture, Ethereum Layer 2, ZK-Rollups, AVC consensus, three-layer blockchain, L2 architecture, L3 DAG, enterprise blockchain]

pages.name: architecture
pages.title: Architecture
pages.content:
> JMDT is a multi-layered blockchain infrastructure designed to support **scalable, privacy-preserving**, and **enterprise-grade data processing** вҖ” from user consent to Ethereum finality.

The architecture is divided into three core layers: **L3** (enterprise DAGs), **L2** (zkRollup + AVC consensus), and **L1** (Ethereum anchoring).

pages.name: architecture
pages.title: Architecture Overview
pages.content:
```mermaid
graph TD
    subgraph L3["Layer 3 вҖ” Enterprise DAG & Consent Infrastructure"]
        A1["Enterprise DAG Nodes (per org/dApp)"]
        A2["Pub/Sub DAG Mesh"]
        A3["InterDAG Bridge"]
        A4["Smart Contracts for Consent & Access"]
        A1 --> A2
        A2 --> A3
    end
    style L3 fill:#1a1a2e,stroke:#71a2e6,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff

    subgraph L2["Layer 2 вҖ” ZK-Enabled State & Rollup Layer"]
        B1["zk Engine (SNARK + STARK)"]
        B2["DID Engine"]
        B3["AVC Consensus Mechanism"]
        B4["Sequencer + MemPool"]
        B5["RISC Zero zkVM"]
        B6["JMDN (JMDT Decentralised Node)"]
        B3 --> B4
        B4 --> B5
        B6 --> B3
    end
    style L2 fill:#0d0d1a,stroke:#8074d9,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff

    subgraph L1["Layer 1 вҖ” Ethereum Settlement"]
        C1["JMDT Smart Contracts"]
        C2["L1 Finality Commit"]
        C1 --> C2
    end
    style L1 fill:#050508,stroke:#df77a8,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff

    L3 -->|"DAG state transitions committed as zk-proofs"| L2
    L2 -->|"STARK proofs submitted for L1 anchoring"| L1
```

pages.name: architecture
pages.title: Layer 3 вҖ” Enterprise DAG & Consent Infrastructure
pages.content: Layer 3 is the enterprise data layer. Each organisation or dApp (e.g., SuperJ, Hercules, finance, healthcare) deploys a **private DAG (Directed Acyclic Graph) node** for high-throughput, localised operations.

pages.name: architecture
pages.title: Enterprise DAG Nodes
pages.content:
- DAG nodes are synchronised internally using **RAFT consensus**, while state transitions are periodically committed to L2
- Supports **10,000+ TPS** for enterprise-scale data operations
- Each node is permissioned, independently scalable, and anchored to JMDT Layer 2 for rollup-based finality and zk-proof auditability

pages.name: architecture
pages.title: Smart Contracts for Consent & Access
pages.content:
- Govern user onboarding, consent capture, and access rights for data exchange
- Only anonymised data is published to DAGs post-consent

pages.name: architecture
pages.title: InterDAG Bridge
pages.content:
- Facilitates cross-enterprise collaboration using shared smart contracts
- Enables access requests, logging, and secure off-chain queries between DAG nodes

pages.name: architecture
pages.title: Pub/Sub DAG Mesh
pages.content:
- Supports streaming analytics and real-time data ingestion via Pub/Sub architecture
- Every node builds **vertices and branches**, logged with persistent storage and synchronised using RAFT

pages.name: architecture
pages.title: Layer 2 вҖ” ZK-Enabled State & Rollup Layer
pages.content: Layer 2 is the core JMDT chain вҖ” where consensus is reached, identities are verified, and zk-proofs are generated and aggregated before Ethereum settlement.

pages.name: architecture
pages.title: zk Engine (SNARK + STARK)
pages.content:
Verifies DAG transactions and batch validity using zero-knowledge proofs. JMDT supports both:
- **zk-SNARKs** вҖ” efficient, minimal proof size for transaction-level validation
- **zk-STARKs** вҖ” quantum-resistant, no trusted setup, for large-scale state aggregation

All ZK circuits are written in **Rust** and executed within the **RISC Zero zkVM**, providing deterministic, auditable guest programs that output STARK proofs submitted to Ethereum.

pages.name: architecture
pages.title: DID Engine
pages.content: Provides W3C-compliant Decentralised Identity, allowing private yet verifiable authentication across all L2 and L3 operations.

pages.name: architecture
pages.title: AVC Consensus Mechanism
pages.content:
JMDT's **Asynchronous Validation Consensus (AVC)** combines:
- **VRF-based buddy selection** вҖ” deterministic, randomised validator sets each round
- **Asynchronous quorum validation** вҖ” no global timing required; parallel validation across all JMDN nodes
- **zk-proof enhanced verification** вҖ” each block includes a zk-proof verified independently by all buddies
- **Gossip-based propagation** вҖ” efficient dissemination of transactions, votes, and zk-proofs
- **CRDT-driven conflict resolution** вҖ” convergence guaranteed even under network partitions

See [AVC Consensus вҶ’](/docs/bft) for full details.

pages.name: architecture
pages.title: Sequencer & MemPool
pages.content: Aggregates DAG state transitions into zk-proofs using a modular zkVM. Manages transaction ordering, batching, and DAG-to-L2 relay.

pages.name: architecture
pages.title: RISC Zero zkVM
pages.content: Executes rollup verification logic as a deterministic, auditable guest program. Outputs STARK proofs submitted to Ethereum for L1 anchoring.

pages.name: architecture
pages.title: JMDN вҖ” JMDT Decentralised Node
pages.content: The **JMDT Decentralised Node (JMDN)** is the primary network node binary. All JMDN nodes participate in AVC consensus, propagate blocks and votes via the Gossip Protocol, and maintain the immudb-backed immutable ledger.

pages.name: architecture
pages.title: Layer 1 вҖ” Ethereum Settlement Layer
pages.content: Ethereum serves as the **foundational settlement layer** for JMDT, providing censorship-resistance, security, and global finality.

pages.name: architecture
pages.title: JMDT Smart Contracts
pages.content:
- Receive L2 commitments, verify zkVM STARK proofs, and finalise state on Ethereum
- Audited by independent firms prior to mainnet deployment
- Open-sourced for full transparency

pages.name: architecture
pages.title: L1 Finality Commit
pages.content:
- Ensures integrity and auditability of enterprise and validator activity
- Supports transparent public settlement while preserving L3 data privacy
- All L2 transactions committed to Ethereum dynamically based on optimal gas fees and block time

pages.name: architecture
pages.title: Key Properties
pages.content:
| Property | Description |
|---|---|
| **Privacy-Preserving** | DID verification, ZK proof data validation вҖ” personal data is verified without being shared |
| **Enterprise-First** | L3 DAG Mesh Network allows private computation, internal tokenisation, and industry-specific logic |
| **Verifiable Rollups** | RISC Zero zkRollups provide immutable, trust-minimised proofs of all activity |
| **Scalable** | L2: 2,000+ TPS; L3 DAG: 10,000+ TPS; ~3вҖ“10s L2 finality |
| **Composable** | Every layer is independently upgradeable and EVM-compatible |

pages.name: architecture
pages.title: Trust Model
pages.content:
| Layer | Trust Model | Threats Mitigated |
|---|---|---|
| **L1 (Ethereum)** | Ethereum finality and censorship-resistance | Network reorgs, malicious smart contracts |
| **L2 (JMDT Chain)** | вүҘ 2/3 honest, randomised validators in AVC | Sybil attacks, block manipulation, equivocation |
| **L3 (Enterprise DAG)** | Internal integrity within enterprise | Insider tampering, unauthorised data access |
| **ZK Proof System** | Soundness of zkSNARK/zkSTARK cryptographic assumptions | Proof forgery, data leakage |
| **RISC Zero zkVM** | Deterministic Rust guest execution | Non-determinism, circuit manipulation |

pages.name: avc
pages.parentTitle: AVC Module вҖ” Asynchronous Validation Consensus
pages.title: AVC Module
pages.content:
> *The Truth Layer for Verifiable Information вҖ” powered by Asynchronous Validation Consensus.*

The **AVC (Asynchronous Validation Consensus)** module is the consensus core of the JMDT network. It implements Byzantine Fault Tolerance (BFT), randomised buddy node selection via VRF, BLS cryptographic signing, and CRDT-based state synchronisation вҖ” all running inside the **JMDN** (JMDT Decentralised Node) binary.

See [AVC Consensus Mechanism вҶ’](/docs/bft) for the protocol-level overview.

pages.name: avc
pages.parentTitle: AVC Module вҖ” Asynchronous Validation Consensus
pages.title: Overview
pages.content:
The AVC module ensures secure, distributed consensus by:

- Implementing BFT consensus protocols for block validation
- Selecting and managing buddy nodes for consensus participation
- Providing BLS cryptographic signing and verification
- Managing voting mechanisms for network decisions
- Synchronising state with CRDT convergence guarantees

pages.name: avc
pages.parentTitle: AVC Module вҖ” Asynchronous Validation Consensus
pages.title: 1. BFT (Byzantine Fault Tolerance)
pages.content:
**Location:** `JMDN/AVC/BFT/`

Implements a Byzantine Fault Tolerant consensus protocol for validating blocks and reaching agreement among network participants.

**Key Features:**
- PREPARE and COMMIT phases for consensus
- Byzantine fault detection and handling
- GossipSub-based message passing
- gRPC communication with Sequencer

**Key Files:**
- `bft/bft.go` вҖ” Main BFT consensus logic
- `bft/engine.go` вҖ” PREPARE and COMMIT phase execution
- `bft/byzantine.go` вҖ” Byzantine fault detection
- `bft/buddy_service.go` вҖ” gRPC server for buddy nodes
- `bft/sequencer_client.go` вҖ” Client for Sequencer communication
- `network/libp2p_setup.go` вҖ” Network setup for BFT

**Documentation:** See `JMDN/AVC/BFT/readme-bft.md` for full BFT implementation details.

pages.parentTitle: AVC Module вҖ” Asynchronous Validation Consensus
pages.title: 2. BLS (Boneh-Lynn-Shacham) Signatures
pages.content:
**Location:** `JMDN/AVC/BLS/`

Provides BLS signature generation and verification for all cryptographic consensus operations.

**Key Features:**
- BLS signature generation and aggregation
- Signature verification across buddy committees
- Router for signature routing

**Key Files:**
- `bls-sign/bls-sign.go` вҖ” BLS signature implementation
- `Router/Router.go` вҖ” Signature routing logic

pages.parentTitle: AVC Module вҖ” Asynchronous Validation Consensus
pages.title: 3. BuddyNodes
pages.content:
**Location:** `JMDN/AVC/BuddyNodes/`

Manages buddy node selection, communication, and consensus participation within each AVC round.

**Key Features:**
- Buddy node selection and management
- Message passing between buddy nodes
- CRDT synchronisation for buddy node state
- PubSub integration for consensus messaging
- BLS signing and verification for buddy messages

**Key Components:**
- `MessagePassing/` вҖ” Communication between buddy nodes
- `CRDTSync/` вҖ” CRDT synchronisation for buddy node state
- `ServiceLayer/` вҖ” Service layer for buddy node operations
- `DataLayer/` вҖ” Data layer for buddy node storage

**Key Files:**
- `MessagePassing/BuddyNodeStream.go` вҖ” Stream handling for buddy messages
- `MessagePassing/MessageListener.go` вҖ” Listens for buddy node messages
- `MessagePassing/CRDTSyncHandler.go` вҖ” Handles CRDT synchronisation

**Location:** `JMDN/AVC/NodeSelection/`

Implements Verifiable Random Function (VRF)-based node selection for choosing buddy node committees each consensus round.

**Key Features:**

pages.name: bft
pages.title: AVC Consensus Mechanism
pages.content:
id: bft
title: AVC Consensus Mechanism
sidebar_label: AVC Consensus
description: JMDT's Asynchronous Validation Consensus (AVC) вҖ” a quorum-based, non-synchronous consensus model using VRF-based buddy selection, zk-proof validation, Gossip Protocol, CRDT conflict resolution, and immudb for tamper-proof finality.
keywords: [AVC consensus, Asynchronous Validation Consensus, JMDT consensus, VRF buddy selection, BuddyVote, CRDT, immudb, JMDN, zk-proof consensus]

pages.name: bft
pages.title: AVC Consensus Mechanism
pages.content:
> **JMDT вҖ” The Truth Layer for Verifiable Information.** To achieve fast, tamper-resistant, and scalable block finality in the JMDT Layer 2 blockchain, we implement **Asynchronous Validation Consensus (AVC)** вҖ” a quorum-based, non-synchronous consensus model designed for decentralised environments and strengthened with zk-proof guarantees.

Unlike producer-driven consensus models, AVC relies on **collective validation by randomised buddy sets**, ensuring fairness, resilience, and transparency. JMDN nodes independently verify proposed blocks, align on Merkle digests, and reach quorum **without relying on fixed committees or synchronous rounds**.

pages.name: bft
pages.title: 1. Asynchronous Validation
pages.content: When the Sequencer proposes a block, **all JMDN (JMDT Decentralised Node) nodes immediately enter validation mode**. This event-driven, non-blocking design allows **parallel validation** across the network without reliance on global timing or designated leaders.

pages.name: bft
pages.title: 2. VRF-Based Buddy Node Selection
pages.content:
A **Verifiable Random Function (VRF)-based Buddy Node Selection Algorithm**, weighted by Seed Node feedback, deterministically selects a buddy set of *k* nodes each round.

- Ensures **fairness, geo-diversity, and resistance to Sybil or cartel attacks**
- Buddies perform: signature and DID checks, balance sufficiency and ownership validation, and zk-proof verification for batch integrity
- A block is accepted when **вүҘ q_buddy = вҢҲ2k/3вҢү buddies** sign and broadcast a `BuddyVote(digest)`
- This threshold guarantees overlap between quorums and resilience against Byzantine behaviour

pages.name: bft
pages.title: 3. zk-Proof Integrity
pages.content:
Each block must include a **zk-proof** (generated off-chain by the RISC Zero zkVM or a prover service):

- zk-proofs validate batched execution correctness and are verified independently by all buddies
- Ensures **privacy-preserving validation** without exposing internal state transitions
- Proofs are generated using Rust-based circuits compiled to zkVM-executable guest binaries

pages.name: bft
pages.title: 4. Gossip Protocol
pages.content:
A decentralised backbone for **disseminating transactions, buddy votes, and zk-proofs** efficiently across the JMDN network:

- **Bloom Filters** prevent duplicate or replayed messages, reducing bandwidth overhead
- Enables low-latency, fault-tolerant propagation of consensus-critical data across all peers

pages.name: bft
pages.title: 5. CRDT-Based Conflict Resolution
pages.content:
If buddy digests diverge, **Conflict-Free Replicated Data Type (CRDT)-based reconciliation** merges results to achieve eventual consistency:

- A temporary scoped leader may coordinate the merge, but no permanent authority exists
- Guarantees convergence even under network partitions
- Used for votes, digests, and mempool views across buddy and network states

pages.name: bft
pages.title: 6. Immutable Ledger via immudb
pages.content:
Finalised blocks are committed to **immudb**, ensuring:
- **Append-only, tamper-proof history**
- **Auditability** for enterprise use cases
- Separate storage of flagged or invalid transactions for accountability

pages.name: bft
pages.title: Consensus Flow
pages.content:
```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant S as Sequencer
    participant JMDN as JMDN Nodes
    participant Buddies as Buddy Set (VRF-selected)
    participant immudb as immudb Ledger
    participant L1 as Ethereum L1

    S->>JMDN: Propose Block + zk-proof
    JMDN->>Buddies: Gossip Block + zk-proof
    Buddies->>Buddies: Verify DID, signatures, zk-proof
    Buddies->>JMDN: Broadcast BuddyVote(digest)
    JMDN->>JMDN: Count votes вҖ” quorum >= ceil(2k/3)?
    JMDN->>immudb: Commit finalised block (append-only)
    JMDN->>L1: Submit STARK proof to Ethereum
```

pages.name: bft
pages.title: Security & Operational Benefits
pages.content:
| Feature | Benefit |
|---|---|
| **zk-proof validation** | Privacy-preserving and verifiable transaction proofs |
| **VRF + quorum** | Randomised, fair buddy sets; strong Byzantine fault tolerance |
| **Gossip + Bloom Filters** | Efficient, low-latency peer-to-peer communication |
| **CRDT-based reconciliation** | Convergent state even under partitions |
| **Incentive alignment** | Sequencer rewarded only after quorum validation succeeds |
| **Adaptive weighting** | Seed Nodes penalise malicious nodes and reward honest ones |

pages.name: bft
pages.title: Why AVC Matters
pages.content:
The AVC protocol ensures that block finality in JMDT is:

- **Verifiable** вҖ” through zk-proofs on every block
- **Decentralised** вҖ” randomised buddy-set validation; no fixed validators
- **Responsive** вҖ” asynchronous quorum, no global coordination (~3вҖ“10s finality)
- **Audit-ready** вҖ” immudb-backed append-only history
- **Adaptive** вҖ” dynamic Seed Node weight updates favour reliable nodes

This design also forms the foundation for **Layer 3 DAG extensions** and enterprise-specific consensus frameworks, making AVC a **future-proof consensus model** for data-driven blockchain applications.

pages.name: bft
pages.title: Implementation Reference
pages.content:
The AVC module lives at `JMDN/AVC/` in the node codebase:

```
JMDN/AVC/
в”ңв”Җв”Җ BFT/              # BuddyVote PREPARE/COMMIT phases, Byzantine fault detection
в”ңв”Җв”Җ BLS/              # BLS signature generation, aggregation, and verification
в”ңв”Җв”Җ BuddyNodes/       # Buddy node selection, CRDT sync, message passing
в”Ӯ   в”ңв”Җв”Җ MessagePassing/
в”Ӯ   в”ңв”Җв”Җ CRDTSync/
в”Ӯ   в””в”Җв”Җ ServiceLayer/
в”ңв”Җв”Җ NodeSelection/    # VRF-based buddy selection (pkg/selection/vrf.go)
в””в”Җв”Җ VoteModule/       # Vote validation and aggregation
```

**Key configuration constants** (`config/constants.go`):
- `MaxMainPeers` вҖ” Maximum main buddy nodes (default: 13)
- `MaxBackupPeers` вҖ” Maximum backup buddy nodes (default: 10)
- `ConsensusTimeout` вҖ” Timeout for consensus operations (default: 20s)

See [JMDN Node вҶ’](/docs/jmdt-node) and [Sequencer вҶ’](/docs/sequencer) for integration details.

pages.title: Comparison: REST vs gRPC

pages.content: The CLI module provides a command-line interface and gRPC server for managing and interacting with the JMZK decentralized network node. It offers both interactive and programmatic access to node operations.

pages.content:

- DID operations

pages.title: 1. Interactive CLI
pages.content:
**File:** `CLI.go`

Provides an interactive command-line interface with the following commands:

**Peer Management:**
- `addpeer <multiaddr>`: Add a peer to managed nodes
- `removepeer <peer_id>`: Remove a peer from managed nodes
- `listpeers`: List all managed peers
- `cleanpeers`: Clean offline peers
- `addrs`: Show node addresses

**Messaging:**
- `msg <peer_multiaddr> <message>`: Send message via libp2p
- `ygg <peer_multiaddr|ygg_ipv6> <message>`: Send message via Yggdrasil
- `broadcast <message>`: Broadcast message to all peers
- `file <peer_multiaddr> <filepath> <remote_filename>`: Send file to peer

**Network Operations:**
- `stats`: Show messaging statistics
- `dbstate`: Show database state
- `fastsync <peer_multiaddr>`: Fast sync with peer
- `syncinfo`: Show FastSync configuration

**DID Operations:**
- `propagateDID <did> <public_key> [balance]`: Propagate DID to network
- `getDID <did>`: Get DID document

**Discovery:**
- `listaliases`: List all peer aliases from seed node
- `discoverneighbors`: Discover and add neighbors from seed node
- `seednodeStats`: Check seed node connection and statistics
- `mempoolStats`: Show mempool statistics

**System:**
- `gethstatus`: Show gETH server status (chain ID, ports)
- `stopservice`: Stop the service and exit
- `help`: Show help message

pages.name: crdt
pages.title: Overview
pages.content: The CRDT (Conflict-Free Replicated Data Types) module provides distributed data structures that ensure eventual consistency across network nodes. It supports offline/online synchronization scenarios where nodes can operate independently and sync when reconnected.

pages.name: crdt
pages.title: Purpose
pages.content:
The CRDT module enables:
- Conflict-free data replication across network nodes
- Offline/online synchronization capabilities
- Eventual consistency guarantees
- Deterministic conflict resolution
- Efficient data synchronization using HashMaps and IBLT

pages.name: crdt
pages.title: вң… **Offline/Online Sync**
pages.content:
- Nodes can go offline and continue operating independently
- Operations are stored for replay when nodes reconnect
- Automatic synchronization on reconnection
- Eventual consistency across all nodes

pages.name: crdt
pages.title: вң… **CRDT Types**
pages.content:
- **LWW-Set (Last-Writer-Wins Set)**: For concurrent add/remove operations
- **G-Counter**: Growing counter for metrics and statistics
- **Vector Clocks**: For causality tracking between distributed events

pages.name: crdt
pages.title: вң… **Synchronization Mechanisms**
pages.content:
- **HashMap-based Diff**: Efficient identification of missing data
- **IBLT (Invertible Bloom Lookup Table)**: Set reconciliation for large datasets
- **Batch Processing**: Optimized data transfer in batches

pages.name: crdt
pages.title: 1. Core CRDT Engine
pages.content:
**File:** `crdt.go`

Main CRDT implementation with:
- LWW-Set operations (Add, Remove, Contains)
- G-Counter operations (Increment, Decrement, Get)
- Vector clock management

pages.content:

Database integration for CRDT persistence:

- Transaction support for atomic operations

pages.title: 3. Memory Store
pages.content:
**File:** `MemoryStore.go`

In-memory storage for CRDT operations:
- Fast in-memory operations
- Efficient data structures
- Memory management

pages.title: 4. HashMap
pages.content:
**File:** `HashMap/`

HashMap implementation for data reconciliation:
- Efficient diff calculation
- Missing data identification
- Batch synchronization

pages.name: crdt
pages.title: 5. IBLT (Invertible Bloom Lookup Table)
pages.content:
**File:** `IBLT/`

IBLT implementation for set reconciliation:
- Efficient set difference calculation
- Large dataset handling
- Network-efficient synchronization

pages.title: 6. Helper Functions
pages.content:
**File:** `helper.go`

Utility functions for CRDT operations:
- Serialization/deserialization
- Data conversion
- Validation

pages.title: LWW-Set (Last-Writer-Wins Set)

pages.name: crdt
pages.title: G-Counter (Growing Counter)
pages.content:
Monotonic counter that only increases:

```go
// Increment counter
crdt.GCounterIncrement("node1", "likes", vectorClock)

// Get counter value
value := crdt.GCounterGet("likes")
```

pages.title: Vector Clocks

pages.name: crdt
pages.title: Basic CRDT Operations

pages.name: crdt
pages.title: Offline/Online Sync

pages.name: crdt
pages.title: Database Integration

pages.name: crdt
pages.title: Phase 1: State Exchange
pages.content: Nodes exchange current CRDT states and vector clocks.

pages.name: crdt
pages.title: Phase 2: Diff Calculation
pages.content: Use HashMaps or IBLT to identify missing operations.

pages.name: crdt
pages.title: Phase 3: Operation Transfer
pages.content: Transfer missing operations in optimized batches.

pages.name: crdt
pages.title: Phase 4: Merge and Apply
pages.content: Merge operations using CRDT semantics and apply results.

pages.name: crdt
pages.title: FastSync Module
pages.content:
- Uses CRDT for blockchain state synchronization
- Efficient diff calculation for missing blocks

pages.name: crdt
pages.title: BuddyNodes Module
pages.content:
- CRDT synchronization for buddy node state
- Consensus state replication

pages.name: crdt
pages.title: Database (DB_OPs)
pages.content:
- Persists CRDT state
- Loads CRDT state for recovery

pages.name: crdt
pages.title: Configuration
pages.content:
Key configuration:
- Memory limits for in-memory operations
- Batch sizes for synchronization
- Timeout values for sync operations

pages.name: crdt
pages.title: Performance
pages.content:
- **Efficient Merging**: O(n) complexity for CRDT merge
- **Network Efficient**: Only missing data is transferred
- **Memory Efficient**: Configurable memory limits
- **Scalable**: Works with any number of nodes

pages.name: crdt
pages.title: Testing
pages.content:
Test files:
- `crdt_test.go`: Core CRDT tests
- `HashMap/`: HashMap tests
- `IBLT/`: IBLT tests

**Test Scenarios:**
- `TestOfflineOnlineSync`: Complete offline/online cycle
- `TestLWWSet`: LWW-Set operations
- `TestGCounter`: G-Counter operations
- `TestVectorClocks`: Vector clock causality

pages.name: crdt
pages.title: Current Limitations
pages.content:
1. **Memory-Only**: Operations stored in memory (not persisted to disk)
2. **Simple Sync**: No sophisticated sync protocols (like Merkle trees)
3. **No Compression**: All operations stored individually
4. **No Garbage Collection**: Operation logs grow indefinitely

pages.name: crdt
pages.title: Future Enhancements
pages.content:
1. **Disk Persistence**: Store operations to disk for true offline capability
2. **Incremental Sync**: Only sync changed data since last sync
3. **Compression**: Compress operation logs to save space
4. **Garbage Collection**: Clean up old operations safely
5. **Network Protocols**: Add real network communication
6. **Conflict Resolution UI**: Show users what conflicts were resolved

pages.name: crdt
pages.title: Real-World Applications
pages.content:
This offline/online capability enables:
- **Mobile Apps**: Work offline, sync when connected
- **Distributed Systems**: Handle network partitions gracefully
- **Edge Computing**: Sync with central systems when available
- **Collaborative Tools**: Multiple users working independently

pages.name: crdt
pages.title: Documentation
pages.content: See `crdt/readme.md` for detailed documentation on offline/online sync capabilities.

pages.name: crdt
pages.title: Conclusion
pages.content:
The CRDT system demonstrates **true distributed system capabilities** with offline/online sync. This provides a production-ready distributed data structure that can handle real-world network conditions and node failures.

The key insight is that **CRDTs make offline/online sync natural** - there's no complex conflict resolution needed because the data structures themselves are designed to handle concurrent modifications gracefully.

- `DIDStats`: Statistics message

pages.name: did-registry
pages.title: DIDCreatorLite Service
pages.content: `DIDCreatorLite.py` exposes a lightweight Flask API for creating, recovering, converting, and resolving Jupiter Meta DIDs. The sections below describe how the service works and how to integrate with each endpoint.

pages.name: did-registry
pages.title: Task Tracker
pages.content: - [x] Document `DIDCreatorLite.py` behavior and endpoints

pages.name: did-registry
pages.title: Overview
pages.content:
- Flask API that issues and resolves Jupiter Meta DIDs.
- Depends on helper modules: `registerDID`, `ethDID`, and `grpcServices`.
- Exposes CORS with origins from `ALLOWED_ORIGINS` (defaults to `*`).
- Optional rate limit (default 5,000 requests per minute per IP) via the `rate_limit` decorator.
- Loads configuration from `.env`; `FLASK_DEBUG` controls debug mode.

pages.name: did-registry
pages.title: Running the Service
pages.content:
1. Install dependencies: `pip install -r requirements.txt`.
2. Configure environment variables (example in `.env`):
   - `ALLOWED_ORIGINS`
   - `FLASK_DEBUG`
   - Any values needed by `register_did` or `generate_eth_did`.
3. Start the API: `python DIDCreatorLite.py`. It listens on `0.0.0.0:5001`.

pages.name: did-registry
pages.title: Common Validations
pages.content:
- `validate_eth_address(address)`: ensures checksum-style `0x` prefixed 40 hex chars.
- `validate_network(network)`: must be one of `mainnet`, `testnet`, `metamask`, `superj`, `iskcon`, `iskconMayapur`, `superjMaster`, `sequencer`.
- `validate_mnemonic(mnemonic)`: checks English BIP-39 mnemonics.

pages.name: did-registry
pages.title: POST `/create_did`
pages.content:
Creates a brand-new DID by generating an Ethereum-style DID with `generate_eth_did`.

**Request body**
- `network` (optional, default `mainnet`): must pass `validate_network`.

**Response**
- `201` with `{ "did": "...", "eth_address": "...", "mnemonic": "...", ... }` from `generate_eth_did`.
- `400` on invalid network or missing payload.
- `500` if DID generation or registration fails (`register_did` invoked with DID and associated Ethereum address).

pages.name: did-registry
pages.title: POST `/recover_did`
pages.content:
Reconstructs the DID from a mnemonic for a given network.

**Request body**
- `mnemonic` (required): validated via `validate_mnemonic`.
- `network` (optional, default `mainnet`): validated network.

**Response**
- `201` with regenerated DID data after re-registering it.
- `400` if mnemonic or network invalid.
- `500` on internal errors.

pages.name: did-registry
pages.title: POST `/convertDID`
pages.content:
Creates a DID directly from an existing Ethereum wallet address.

**Request body**
- `wallet` (required): Ethereum address.
- `network` (optional, default `metamask`).

**Response**
- `200` with `{ "status": true, "didResponse": { ... } }` where `didResponse` is from `generate_did`.
- `400` if wallet or network invalid.
- `500` on internal errors.

pages.name: did-registry
pages.title: POST `/resolve_did`
pages.content:
Fetches on-chain/off-chain DID document data.

**Request body**
- `did` (required string).

**Response**
- `200` with lookup result from `get_did`.
- `400` if DID missing/invalid.
- `404` if `get_did` returns nothing.
- `500` on internal errors.

pages.name: did-registry
pages.title: Supporting Helpers
pages.content:
- `generate_did(network, account)`: builds `did:jmdt:{network}:{account[2:]}` then registers it with public key placeholder `"None"`. Used by `/convertDID`.
- `register_did` and `get_did` are imported from `registerDID.py` and handle persistence/lookup.

pages.name: did-registry
pages.title: Summary
pages.content: Documented the DIDCreatorLite Flask service, covering setup requirements, validation logic, all four HTTP endpoints, and helper behavior for quick onboarding.

pages.title: How to Get JMDT
pages.content:
> *The Truth Layer for Verifiable Information вҖ” JMDT is the native token powering the JMDT Layer 2 network.*

JMDT is the utility token of the JMDT Layer 2 вҖ” used for gas fees, staking, governance, and accessing enterprise data services. You can acquire JMDT on mainnet via a centralised exchange, or claim testnet tokens from the upcoming faucet.

pages.name: get-jmdt
pages.title: Mainnet вҖ” Buy on Bitmart
pages.content: JMDT is listed on **[Bitmart](https://www.bitmart.com)**, a globally accessible centralised exchange. Bitmart supports trading pairs, spot purchases, and withdrawals to self-custodial wallets.

pages.name: get-jmdt
pages.title: Steps to Purchase
pages.content:
1. **Create a Bitmart account** at [bitmart.com](https://www.bitmart.com) if you don't already have one
2. **Complete KYC** (identity verification) as required by Bitmart
3. **Deposit funds** вҖ” use supported fiat on-ramps or deposit USDT/BTC/ETH
4. **Search for JMDT** in the Bitmart trading interface
5. **Place a buy order** вҖ” market or limit, depending on your preference
6. **Withdraw to your wallet** вҖ” send your JMDT to a self-custodial EVM-compatible wallet (e.g., MetaMask) for use on the JMDT network

> **Tip:** JMDT is an ERC-20 compatible token. Ensure your wallet supports EVM networks and that you are sending to the correct network address.

pages.name: get-jmdt
pages.title: Testnet вҖ” Faucet (Coming Soon)
pages.content:
If you are a developer building on JMDT or testing node operations, you will be able to claim **free testnet JMDT** from the official JMDT faucet.

**The testnet faucet is coming soon.** Once live, you will be able to:

1. Connect your EVM-compatible wallet
2. Request a testnet JMDT allocation
3. Start testing smart contracts, DID flows, and enterprise DAG integrations on the JMDT testnet

Watch the [JMDT website](https://jmdt.io) and official channels for the faucet launch announcement.

pages.name: get-jmdt
pages.title: Token Utility
pages.content:
Once you have JMDT, here is what you can do with it:

| Use Case | Description |
|---|---|
| **Gas fees** | Pay for transactions on the JMDT Layer 2 |
| **Staking** | Stake JMDT to participate in AVC consensus as a validator |
| **Governance** | Vote on protocol upgrades and network parameters |
| **Enterprise access** | Pay for enterprise DAG node allocations and data services |
| **DeFi** | Use JMDT in DeFi protocols built on the JMDT L2 |

For full token economics, see [Tokenomics вҶ’](/docs/tokenomics).

pages.name: get-jmdt
pages.title: Security Reminders
pages.content:
- Only purchase JMDT from official, verified sources вҖ” always verify contract addresses from the [official JMDT website](https://jmdt.io)
- Be cautious of phishing sites or unofficial "JMDT faucet" pages before the official faucet launches
- Never share your private keys or seed phrase with anyone

pages.name: intro
pages.title: Introduction
pages.content: > **JMDT is the Truth Layer for all information** вҖ” restoring authenticity in digital infrastructure by privately verifying humans and decentralising their data.

pages.name: intro
pages.title: What is Jupiter Meta Data Token (JMDT)?
pages.content:
**Jupiter Meta Data Token (JMDT)** is a modular, Ethereum-based **Layer 2 (L2) blockchain protocol** designed to address the scalability, privacy, and compliance limitations of traditional blockchain systems.

Built with **Zero-Knowledge Proofs (ZKPs)**, **Decentralized Identity (DID)**, and the proprietary **Asynchronous Validation Consensus (AVC)**, JMDT delivers a high-performance, privacy-preserving infrastructure tailored for both decentralized applications and enterprise-grade solutions.

JMDT's architecture spans three layers:

- **L3 DAG Layer** вҖ” enterprise-grade, high-throughput data operations (10,000+ TPS)
- **L2 Rollup Layer** вҖ” privacy-preserving execution and zk-based consensus (2,000+ TPS, ~3вҖ“10s finality)
- **L1 Finality Layer** вҖ” Ethereum anchoring and global settlement

pages.name: intro
pages.title: Why Choose JMDT?
pages.content:
Conventional blockchains struggle with performance, privacy, and real-world compliance. JMDT addresses these through a tightly integrated architecture:

- **Zero-Knowledge Proofs (ZKPs):** Enabling identity verification, private transaction validation, and 100% data privacy through trustless infrastructure. JMDT supports both **zk-SNARKs** and **zk-STARKs** via the **RISC Zero zkVM**, with ZK circuits authored in Rust for auditability and reproducibility.

- **Decentralized Identity (DID):** W3C-compliant, privacy-preserving authentication that verifies PII with on-chain records without accessing any personal information. Supports self-sovereign, enterprise-managed, and service-issued DIDs.

- **Layer 2 Scaling:** High-throughput zkRollup engine built using RISC Zero zkVM for verifiable Rust-based execution. Designed for faster settlements and large-scale concurrent transactions.

- **AVC Consensus:** JMDT's **Asynchronous Validation Consensus** delivers:
  - Scalable and secure block finality via quorum-based buddy voting
  - Energy-efficient operation by eliminating mining
  - Low-latency, fault-tolerant propagation via Gossip Protocol
  - Seamless compatibility with DAG-based L3 enterprise extensions

- **Ethereum Interoperability:** Full EVM compatibility вҖ” deploy existing Ethereum smart contracts on JMDT without modification. Supports DeFi, NFT marketplaces, DAOs, and enterprise solutions.

- **Immutable Storage via immudb:** Tamper-proof, append-only audit trails across all data and DAG activity.

- **Privacy-Preserving Queries:** DID-based access with zk-proofed data capture вҖ” enabling GDPR-compliant data sharing without exposing sensitive details.

- **Enterprise Mesh Model:** Supports interoperable DAGs per organisation, enabling modular private ecosystems with shared L2 accountability.

pages.name: intro
pages.title: Core Objectives
pages.content:
| Objective | Description |
|---|---|
| **Scalability** | >2,000 TPS (L2) via zkRollups; 10K+ TPS (L3) via DAG batching |
| **Privacy** | End-to-end privacy via ZKPs and DID-based access controls |
| **Security** | zkVM-based execution, AVC consensus, and stake-based accountability |
| **Interoperability** | Fully compatible with Ethereum and Web3 SDKs |
| **Efficiency** | Bloom Filters reduce propagation redundancy; zkRollups optimise gas fees for L1 finality |
| **Governance** | JMDT token enables on-chain DAO governance and validator staking |

JMDT has cryptographically verified **over 20 million humans** through its ecosystem without exposing their identity вҖ” empowering users to monetise authentic data they actually own, and giving enterprises a verifiable chain of custody from human consent to predictive insight.

pages.content:
id: jmdt-node
title: JMDN вҖ” JMDT Decentralised Node
sidebar_label: JMDN Node
description: The JMDT Decentralised Node (JMDN) вҖ” peer-to-peer communication, blockchain synchronisation, AVC consensus, and resilient messaging for the JMDT Layer 2 network.
keywords: [JMDN, JMDT node, P2P communication, blockchain sync, libp2p, immudb, FastSync, CRDT, AVC consensus, JMDT Layer 2]

pages.name: jmdt-node
pages.title: JMDN вҖ” JMDT Decentralised Node
pages.content:
> *The Truth Layer for Verifiable Information вҖ” every JMDN node anchors verifiable state to the JMDT chain.*

**JMDN** (JMDT Decentralised Node) is the primary network binary for the JMDT Layer 2. It combines peer-to-peer communication, AVC consensus participation, blockchain synchronisation via FastSync, and an immudb-backed immutable ledger вҖ” all in a single, cross-platform binary.

For build and deployment instructions, see [Running a JMDN Node вҶ’](/docs/running-a-node).

pages.name: jmdt-node
pages.title: Architecture
pages.content:
JMDN is built on a modular architecture combining several advanced distributed systems concepts:

- **Libp2p Network Layer** вҖ” Foundation for peer discovery, connection management, and direct messaging
- **immudb Integration** вҖ” Tamper-proof immutable database with Merkle trees for the local ledger
- **FastSync Protocol** вҖ” Efficient blockchain state synchronisation between nodes
- **Gossip Protocol** вҖ” Reliable information dissemination across the JMDT network
- **CRDT Engine** вҖ” Conflict-free replicated data types ensuring eventual consistency
- **AVC Consensus** вҖ” Asynchronous Validation Consensus with VRF-based buddy selection
- **Bloom Filter Optimisation** вҖ” Memory-efficient set membership testing to prevent redundant data transfers

pages.name: jmdt-node
pages.title: Features
pages.content:
- **Decentralised Communication** вҖ” Direct peer-to-peer messaging without central servers
- **Blockchain Synchronisation** вҖ” Efficiently sync chain state between nodes using FastSync
- **Bloom Filter Optimisation** вҖ” Reduces network traffic by identifying missing blocks
- **Web Explorer & API** вҖ” Browser-based block explorer and REST API
- **Heartbeat Monitoring** вҖ” Automatic health checks and peer status tracking
- **Yggdrasil Integration** вҖ” Alternative encrypted mesh networking for improved privacy and connectivity
- **Metrics & Monitoring** вҖ” Prometheus-compatible metrics for system performance
- **Seed Node Discovery** вҖ” Bootstrap mechanism for network peer joining; see [Seednode вҶ’](/docs/seednode)

pages.name: jmdt-node
pages.title: System Requirements
pages.content:
| Component | Minimum |
|---|---|
| OS | Ubuntu 18.04+, Debian 10+, macOS, FreeBSD |
| Architecture | x86_64, ARM64, or ARMv7 |
| RAM | 2 GB |
| Storage | 10 GB free |
| Network | Internet connection |

pages.name: jmdt-node
pages.title: Running a Node
pages.content:
Basic node startup after installation:

```bash
./jmdn
```

pages.name: jmdt-node
pages.title: Configuration Flags
pages.content:
| Flag | Description | Default |
|---|---|---|
| `-seed` | Run as a seed node | `false` |
| `-connect <multiaddr>` | Connect to a seed node | вҖ” |
| `-heartbeat <seconds>` | Heartbeat interval | `120` |
| `-metrics <port>` | Prometheus metrics port | `"8080"` |
| `-logdir <path>` | Log directory | `"./logs"` |
| `-console` | Log to console | `false` |
| `-ygg` | Enable Yggdrasil messaging | `true` |
| `-explorer <port>` | Run block explorer (0=disabled) | `0` |
| `-api <port>` | Run immudb API (0=disabled) | `0` |

pages.name: jmdt-node
pages.title: Node Commands
pages.content:
Once running, the JMDN interactive console supports:

| Command | Description |
|---|---|
| `msg <peer_multiaddr> <message>` | Send a message via libp2p |
| `ygg <peer_multiaddr\|ygg_ipv6> <message>` | Send a message via Yggdrasil |
| `file <peer_multiaddr> <filepath>` | Send a file to a peer |
| `addpeer <peer_multiaddr>` | Add a peer to managed nodes |
| `removepeer <peer_id>` | Remove a peer from managed list |
| `listpeers` | Show all managed peers |
| `peers` | Request updated peer list from seed |
| `stats` | Show messaging statistics |
| `broadcast <message>` | Broadcast to all connected peers |
| `fastsync <peer_multiaddr>` | Fast sync blockchain data with a peer |
| `dbstate` | Show current immudb database state |
| `exit` | Exit the node |

pages.name: jmdt-node
pages.title: FastSync Protocol
pages.content:
FastSync efficiently synchronises blockchain data between nodes using:

1. **Initial State Exchange** вҖ” Nodes exchange current chain height and Merkle roots
2. **Bloom Filter Optimisation** вҖ” Identifies missing blocks using probabilistic data structures
3. **Batch Processing** вҖ” Transfers missing blocks in optimised batches
4. **Verification** вҖ” Final Merkle root comparison to ensure consistency

pages.name: jmdt-node
pages.title: CRDT Implementation
pages.content:
JMDN uses Conflict-free Replicated Data Types (CRDTs) to ensure data consistency across all nodes:

- **Last-Writer-Wins Sets** вҖ” For handling user data with timestamps
- **Counters** вҖ” For metrics and statistics requiring eventual consistency
- **Vector Clocks** вҖ” For causality tracking between distributed events

pages.name: jmdt-node
pages.title: P2P Network Features
pages.content:
- **Automatic Node Discovery** вҖ” Find and connect to other JMDN peers
- **NAT Traversal** вҖ” Connect to peers behind NATs and firewalls
- **Transport Layer Security** вҖ” Encrypted communication between all peers
- **Multi-Protocol Support** вҖ” Both libp2p and Yggdrasil messaging

pages.name: jmdt-node
pages.title: Web Explorer & API
pages.content:
When enabled, JMDN provides:

- **Block Explorer** вҖ” Web interface for viewing chain data at the configured port
- **RESTful API** вҖ” HTTP endpoints for programmatic immudb access
- **Metrics Dashboard** вҖ” Prometheus-compatible performance and health monitoring

pages.name: logging
pages.title: Overview
pages.content: The Logging module provides structured logging capabilities for the JMZK network. It supports both file-based logging and centralized logging via Loki, with async logging for performance.

pages.name: logging
pages.title: Purpose
pages.content:
The Logging module enables:
- Structured logging with Zap logger
- File-based logging
- Centralized logging via Loki
- Async logging for performance
- Log level management
- Topic-based logging

pages.name: logging
pages.title: 1. Async Logger
pages.content:
**File:** `log.go`

Main logging implementation:
- `AsyncLogger`: Async logger with file and Loki support
- `NewAsyncLogger`: Create new async logger
- `ReturnDefaultLogger`: Create default logger
- `ReturnDefaultLoggerWithLoki`: Create logger with Loki support

pages.name: logging
pages.title: 2. Logger Builder
pages.content:
**File:** `Builder.go`

Builder pattern for logger configuration:
- `LoggerBuilder`: Builder for logger configuration
- `NewLoggerBuilder`: Create new logger builder
- `SetFileName`: Set log file name
- `SetTopic`: Set log topic
- `SetURL`: Set Loki URL
- `Build`: Build logger instance

pages.name: logging
pages.title: 3. Log Configuration
pages.content:
**File:** `LogConfig.go`

Logging configuration structures:
- `Logging`: Main logging configuration
- `LoggingMetadata`: Logging metadata configuration
- Log level constants
- Log format configuration

pages.name: logging
pages.title: 4. Loki Integration
pages.content:
**File:** `loki.go`

Loki client integration:
- `lokiWriteSyncer`: Loki write syncer
- `newLokiWriteSyncer`: Create new Loki write syncer
- Batch logging support
- Retry logic

pages.name: logging
pages.title: Create Logger

pages.name: logging
pages.title: Using Logger Builder
pages.content:
```go
// Create logger with builder
logger, err := logging.NewLoggerBuilder().
    SetFileName("app.log").
    SetTopic("app").
    SetURL("http://localhost:3100/loki/api/v1/push").
    Build()
```

pages.name: logging
pages.title: Logging Messages

pages.name: logging
pages.title: Global Logger

pages.name: logging
pages.title: Basic Logging

pages.name: logging
pages.title: Logging with Loki

pages.name: logging
pages.title: Using Logger Builder
pages.content:
```go
import "gossipnode/logging"

// Create logger with builder
logger, err := logging.NewLoggerBuilder().
    SetFileName("app.log").
    SetTopic("app").
    SetDirectory("logs").
    SetBatchSize(100).
    SetBatchWait(2 * time.Second).
    SetTimeout(6 * time.Second).
    SetURL("http://localhost:3100/loki/api/v1/push").
    Build()
if err != nil {
    log.Fatal(err)
}
defer logger.Close()

// Log messages
logger.Logger.Info("Application started")
```

pages.name: logging
pages.title: Global Logger
pages.content:
```go
import "gossipnode/logging"

// Set global logger
logger, _ := logging.ReturnDefaultLogger("app.log", "app")
logging.SetGlobalLogger(logger)

// Use global logger
logging.LogInfo("Message", zap.String("key", "value"))
logging.LogError("Error", zap.Error(err))
```

pages.name: mempool-routing-engine
pages.title: Mempool Routing Engine (MRE)
pages.content: A high-performance control plane for decentralized mempool networks. The MRE provides deterministic transaction placement, replication, consensus, and retrieval across independent mempool nodes with high availability, fault tolerance, and low latency.

pages.name: mempool-routing-engine
pages.title: рҹҡҖ **Modern HRW Integration (v2.0)**
pages.content:
- вң… **HRW Manager**: Complete epoch-based HRW Manager with Redis Streams integration
- вң… **Dynamic Weight Updates**: Automatic HRW weight updates via `ChangeWeightage()` function
- вң… **Redis Streams Publishing**: Real-time transaction placement publishing to Redis Streams
- вң… **Epoch-based Configuration**: Historical transaction stability with dynamic weight updates
- вң… **Multi-format NodeID Support**: Flexible NodeID to ShardID conversion for various naming patterns
- вң… **Weight Scheduler Integration**: Automatic HRW Manager updates when new weights are calculated
- вң… **Reflection-based Integration**: Seamless HRW Manager integration without circular imports

pages.name: mempool-routing-engine
pages.title: рҹ”§ **Enhanced Features**
pages.content:
- вң… **HRW - Weights Algorithm**: Advanced weighted selection based on system metrics, mempool capacity, and fee affordability
- вң… **High Throughput Testing**: Enhanced test clients supporting 1000+ transactions per second
- вң… **Real-time Metrics**: Redis integration for live system and mempool metrics
- вң… **Latency-aware Routing**: Network latency measurement and exclusion of unreachable mempools
- вң… **Weight Scheduler**: Background process for periodic weight updates (configurable interval)
- вң… **Enhanced Go Client**: Multi-worker concurrent transaction testing with real-time statistics
- вң… **Python Test Clients**: Alternative test clients with async support and batch processing
- вң… **Flexible Server URLs**: Command-line URL arguments for testing different environments
- вң… **Integrated MRE Binary**: Single executable with all modules integrated
- вң… **Run-All Command**: Start all modules simultaneously with one command
- вң… **JSON Configuration**: Mempool addresses configurable via JSON file
- вң… **Redis Integration**: Real-time log storage and analytics with Redis
- вң… **Logging Server**: Dedicated NATS consumer with Redis persistence
- вң… **Unified CLI**: Single MRE binary with server, logging, and run-all subcommands
- вң… **Docker Support**: Redis container setup and testing
- вң… **Comprehensive Testing**: Full client-server integration tests

pages.name: mempool-routing-engine
pages.title: рҹҸ—пёҸ Architecture Overview
pages.content:
The Mempool Routing Engine acts as a smart proxy between JMDT (JMDT) nodes and actual mempool servers, providing:

- **Transaction Validation**: Comprehensive Ethereum transaction format validation
- **Modern HRW Manager**: Epoch-based HRW Manager with Redis Streams integration for intelligent routing
- **Dynamic Weight Updates**: Automatic weight updates via `ChangeWeightage()` when new weights are calculated
- **Redis Streams Publishing**: Real-time transaction placement decisions published to Redis Streams
- **Intelligent Routing**: Deterministic placement across multiple mempool nodes with HRW weights algorithm
- **HRW Weights Algorithm**: Advanced weighted selection based on system metrics, mempool capacity, and fee affordability
- **Centralized Logging**: NATS JetStream-based log aggregation and routing
- **Redis Persistence**: Real-time log storage and analytics with Redis
- **High Availability**: Fault-tolerant design with automatic failover
- **Real-time Monitoring**: Comprehensive metrics and health checks

pages.title: Prerequisites
pages.content:
- Go 1.22+
- NATS Server
- Redis Server
- Docker (for Redis container)
- buf (for protobuf generation)

pages.name: mempool-routing-engine
pages.title: рҹҡҖ Quick Start Command
pages.content:
**Start all services with one command:**

```bash

pages.name: mempool-routing-engine
pages.title: Build the MRE binary
pages.content: go build -o MRE .

pages.name: mempool-routing-engine
pages.title: Set up mempool configuration (first time only)
pages.content: cp mempoolAddress.example.json mempoolAddress.json

pages.name: mempool-routing-engine
pages.title: Start all modules simultaneously (RECOMMENDED)
pages.content: ./MRE run-all -server-port=8080 -stream=all

pages.name: mempool-routing-engine
pages.title: Alternative: Start with custom settings
pages.content: ./MRE run-all -server-port=8080 -stream=all -nats-url=nats://localhost:4222 -redis=localhost:6379 -config=custom-mempools.json

pages.name: mempool-routing-engine
pages.title: Use custom mempool configuration
pages.content:
./MRE run-all -config=custom-mempools.json -server-port=8080
```

**What this does:**
- вң… Starts gRPC server on port 8080
- вң… Starts logging server with all streams
- вң… Connects to NATS and Redis
- вң… Loads mempool configurations from `mempoolAddress.json`
- вң… Initializes all configured mempool connections
- вң… Ready to handle transactions immediately

pages.content: The MRE uses a JSON configuration file to manage mempool server connections. This makes it easy to add, remove, or modify mempool servers without changing code.

pages.name: mempool-routing-engine
pages.title: **Configuration Benefits**
pages.content:
- рҹ”§ **Easy Management**: Add/remove mempools without code changes
- рҹҢҚ **Environment Specific**: Different configs for dev/staging/prod
- рҹ“қ **Descriptive**: Each mempool has a description field

pages.title: Command-Line Options
pages.content:
The MRE binary supports multiple subcommands:

```bash

pages.name: mempool-routing-engine
pages.title: Show help message
pages.content: ./MRE help

pages.name: mempool-routing-engine
pages.title: Start routing engine server
pages.content: ./MRE server -port=8080

pages.title: Start server with custom mempool configuration
pages.content: ./MRE server -config=custom-mempools.json -port=8080

pages.name: mempool-routing-engine
pages.title: Start logging server with Redis
pages.content: ./MRE loggingServer -stream=all -redis=localhost:6379

pages.name: mempool-routing-engine
pages.title: Run all modules simultaneously (RECOMMENDED)
pages.content: ./MRE run-all

pages.name: mempool-routing-engine
pages.title: Run all modules with custom configuration
pages.content: ./MRE run-all -config=production-mempools.json -server-port=8080

pages.content:
./MRE server -help
./MRE loggingServer -help
./MRE run-all -help
```

pages.name: mempool-routing-engine
pages.title: Available Subcommands:
pages.content:
**`server`** - Start the gRPC routing engine server
- `-port`: Port to listen on (default: 8080)
- `-config`: Path to mempool configuration JSON file (default: mempoolAddress.json)
- `-weight-interval`: Weight update interval (default: 5m)
- `-help`: Show server help message

**`loggingServer`** - Start the logging server for real-time log monitoring
- `-url`: NATS server URL (default: nats://localhost:4222)
- `-stream`: Stream type to consume (all, system, mempool, app)
- `-redis`: Redis server URL (default: localhost:6379)
- `-redis-password`: Redis password (default: empty)
- `-redis-db`: Redis database number (default: 0)

**`run-all`** - Start both server and logging server simultaneously
- `-server-port`: Port for the gRPC server (default: 8080)
- `-config`: Path to mempool configuration JSON file (default: mempoolAddress.json)
- `-weight-interval`: Weight update interval (default: 5m)
- `-stream`: Stream type for logging server (default: all)
- `-nats-url`: NATS server URL (default: nats://localhost:4222)
- `-redis`: Redis server URL (default: localhost:6379)
- `-redis-password`: Redis password (default: empty)
- `-redis-db`: Redis database number (default: 0)

**`test-weights`** - Test the HRW weights algorithm with real-time metrics
- `-redis`: Redis server URL for real-time metrics (optional)
- Demonstrates weight calculation with live system and mempool data
- Tests HRW Manager integration with dynamic weight updates
- Validates NodeID to ShardID conversion for various formats

pages.name: mempool-routing-engine
pages.title: Quick Start with `run-all`
pages.content:
The easiest way to start the entire MRE system is using the `run-all` command:

```bash

pages.name: mempool-routing-engine
pages.title: Start all modules with default settings
pages.content: ./MRE run-all

pages.name: mempool-routing-engine
pages.title: Start all modules with custom server port
pages.content: ./MRE run-all -server-port=9090

pages.name: mempool-routing-engine
pages.title: Start all modules with custom mempool configuration
pages.content: ./MRE run-all -config=production-mempools.json -server-port=8080

pages.name: mempool-routing-engine
pages.title: Start all modules with custom settings
pages.content: ./MRE run-all -server-port=9090 -stream=system -nats-url=nats://localhost:4222

pages.name: mempool-routing-engine
pages.title: Test the HRW weights algorithm with HRW Manager
pages.content:
./MRE test-weights -redis localhost:6379
```

pages.title: Using Makefile
pages.content: ```bash

pages.name: mempool-routing-engine
pages.title: Start all modules
pages.content: make run-all

pages.name: mempool-routing-engine
pages.title: Start all modules with custom server port
pages.content:
make run-all SERVER_PORT=9090
```

pages.name: mempool-routing-engine
pages.title: What `run-all` Does
pages.content:
The `run-all` command starts both modules simultaneously:

1. **gRPC Server** - Handles transaction routing and validation
2. **Logging Server** - Consumes NATS streams and stores logs in Redis

Both modules run concurrently and can be stopped together with `Ctrl+C`.

pages.content:

```bash

pages.name: mempool-routing-engine
pages.title: Terminal 2 - Start the logging server
pages.content:
./MRE loggingServer -stream=all
```

pages.name: mempool-routing-engine
pages.title: High Throughput Testing
pages.content: The MRE includes comprehensive high throughput testing capabilities to validate performance under load:

pages.name: mempool-routing-engine
pages.title: **Enhanced Go Test Client**
pages.content:
The enhanced `transaction_client.go` now supports high throughput testing with multiple worker goroutines:

```bash

pages.name: mempool-routing-engine
pages.title: High throughput test - 1000 tx/sec for 60 seconds with 10 workers
pages.content: go run transaction_client.go -high-throughput -rate=1000 -duration=60 -workers=10

pages.name: mempool-routing-engine
pages.title: Stress test - 2000 tx/sec for 30 seconds
pages.content: go run transaction_client.go -high-throughput -rate=2000 -duration=30 -workers=20

pages.name: mempool-routing-engine
pages.title: Custom server URL
pages.content: go run transaction_client.go -high-throughput -server=34.174.63.179:8080 -rate=500 -duration=30

pages.name: mempool-routing-engine
pages.title: Batch testing
pages.content: go run transaction_client.go -batch -batch-count=10 -batch-size=100

pages.name: mempool-routing-engine
pages.title: Traditional testing (still available)
pages.content:
go run transaction_client.go -count=50 -delay=500ms
```

pages.name: mempool-routing-engine
pages.title: **Python Test Clients**
pages.content:
Alternative Python clients provide additional testing flexibility:

```bash

pages.name: mempool-routing-engine
pages.title: High throughput Python client (gRPC)
pages.content: python3 high_throughput_client.py --mode throughput --rate=1000 --duration=60 --workers=10

pages.name: mempool-routing-engine
pages.title: Simple Python client (gRPC)
pages.content: python3 simple_python_client.py --mode throughput --rate=1000 --duration=60 --workers=10

pages.name: mempool-routing-engine
pages.title: HTTP-based client (no gRPC dependencies)
pages.content: python3 simple_http_client.py --mode throughput --rate=1000 --duration=60 --workers=10

pages.name: mempool-routing-engine
pages.title: Batch testing
pages.content: python3 high_throughput_client.py --mode batch --batch-size=100 --batches=10

pages.name: mempool-routing-engine
pages.title: Custom server URL
pages.content:
python3 high_throughput_client.py --server=34.174.63.179:8080 --mode throughput --rate=500
```

**Python Client Options:**
- **`high_throughput_client.py`**: Full-featured async client with advanced statistics
- **`simple_python_client.py`**: Basic gRPC client for simple testing
- **`simple_http_client.py`**: HTTP-based client (bypasses gRPC compilation)

See [Tests/PYTHON_README.md](Tests/PYTHON_README.md) for detailed Python client documentation.

pages.name: mempool-routing-engine
pages.title: Transaction Validation
pages.content:
The routing engine validates transactions against these criteria:

- **Required Fields**: Hash, From, To, Value, Type, ChainId, Nonce, GasLimit
- **Format Validation**:
  - Hash: Must start with "0x" and be 66 characters
  - Addresses: Must start with "0x" and be 42 characters
- **Empty Field Detection**: Trims whitespace and rejects empty fields

pages.name: mempool-routing-engine
pages.title: рҹ§® HRW Weights Algorithm
pages.content: The MRE includes an advanced **Highest Random Weight (HRW)** algorithm for intelligent mempool routing. This algorithm calculates dynamic weights for each mempool node based on real-time system metrics, mempool capacity, and fee affordability.

pages.name: mempool-routing-engine
pages.title: рҹҺҜ Algorithm Overview
pages.content:
The HRW weights algorithm provides:

- **Multi-factor Weighting**: Considers system health, mempool capacity, cache performance, and fee affordability
- **Real-time Metrics**: Live data from Redis for system and mempool performance
- **Latency-aware Routing**: Network latency measurement and exclusion of unreachable mempools
- **Dynamic Load Balancing**: Automatically adjusts weights based on real-time metrics
- **CPU Moving Average**: Smooths out CPU spikes for more stable weight calculations
- **Configurable Intervals**: Weight update frequency can be customized (default: 5 minutes)
- **Fee Affordability**: Optional transaction caps for cost-conscious routing
- **Resource Pressure Handling**: Inverted scoring for lower-is-better metrics
- **Strictly Positive Weights**: Ensures consistent hashing for HRW

pages.name: mempool-routing-engine
pages.title: рҹ“Ҡ Weight Calculation Factors
pages.content: The algorithm considers the following metrics:

pages.name: mempool-routing-engine
pages.title: **System Metrics**
pages.content:
- **CPU Usage**: Lower usage = higher weight (with moving average smoothing)
- **Memory Usage**: Lower usage = higher weight  
- **Disk Usage**: Lower usage = higher weight
- **Load Average**: Lower load = higher weight
- **Network Latency**: Lower latency = higher weight (unreachable mempools excluded)

pages.name: mempool-routing-engine
pages.title: **Mempool Metrics**
pages.content:
- **Hot Cache Size**: Available capacity vs. maximum capacity
- **Total Transactions**: Current load vs. maximum capacity
- **Cache Hit Rate**: Performance efficiency (higher = better)
- **Average Fee**: Affordability vs. target fee

pages.name: mempool-routing-engine
pages.title: **Transaction Caps (Optional)**
pages.content:
- **Max Fee**: Maximum fee the transaction can pay
- **Max Priority Fee**: Maximum priority fee for EIP-1559 transactions

pages.name: mempool-routing-engine
pages.title: рҹ”§ Configuration Parameters
pages.content:
The algorithm uses configurable parameters for tuning:

```go
type WeightParams struct {
    // Normalization anchors
    MaxHotCache      float64 // e.g., 5,000
    MaxTotalTx       float64 // e.g., 100,000
    GoodCacheHitPct  float64 // e.g., 95%
    GoodLoadAvg      float64 // e.g., #vCPUs
    TargetAvgFeeWei  float64 // e.g., affordable fee target
    MinReliability   float64 // safety floor, e.g., 0.1

    // Blend weights (automatically normalized)
    WFreeCap         float64 // Free capacity weight (default: 0.30)
    WCacheHit        float64 // Cache hit rate weight (default: 0.15)
    WLowFee          float64 // Low fee weight (default: 0.20)
    WLowCPU          float64 // Low CPU weight (default: 0.12)
    WLowMem          float64 // Low memory weight (default: 0.10)
    WLowDisk         float64 // Low disk weight (default: 0.05)
    WLowLoad         float64 // Low load weight (default: 0.08)

    // Global congestion throttle
    SurgeFactor      float64 // 0..1, usually 1.0
}
```

pages.name: mempool-routing-engine
pages.title: вҡҷпёҸ Weight Scheduler Configuration
pages.content:
The weight scheduler runs in the background and periodically updates mempool weights:

```bash

pages.name: mempool-routing-engine
pages.title: Start with default 5-minute interval
pages.content: ./MRE run-all

pages.name: mempool-routing-engine
pages.title: Start with custom weight update interval (e.g., 2 minutes)
pages.content: ./MRE run-all -weight-interval=2m

pages.name: mempool-routing-engine
pages.title: Start server with custom weight interval
pages.content:
./MRE server -weight-interval=1m -port=8080
```

**Weight Scheduler Features:**
- **Background Process**: Runs concurrently with the main server
- **Configurable Interval**: Set update frequency via command-line argument
- **Real-time Metrics**: Fetches live data from Redis
- **Latency Measurement**: Measures network latency to each mempool
- **Automatic Exclusion**: Skips unreachable or high-latency mempools
- **Detailed Logging**: Comprehensive logs of weight calculations and routing decisions

pages.name: mempool-routing-engine
pages.title: рҹ”„ Integration with Routing
pages.content:
The calculated weights are used for:

1. **Consistent Hashing**: Deterministic mempool selection based on transaction hash
2. **Load Balancing**: Higher weight = higher probability of selection
3. **Performance Optimization**: Routes to best-performing mempools
4. **Cost Optimization**: Considers fee affordability when transaction caps are provided

pages.name: mempool-routing-engine
pages.title: рҹ“Ҳ Performance Benefits
pages.content:
- **Intelligent Routing**: Routes transactions to optimal mempools
- **Load Distribution**: Prevents overloading of individual mempools
- **Cost Efficiency**: Considers fee affordability for cost-conscious routing
- **Real-time Adaptation**: Weights update based on current system state
- **Fault Tolerance**: Automatically reduces weight of unhealthy nodes

pages.name: mempool-routing-engine
pages.title: рҹҡҖ Modern HRW Manager Integration
pages.content: The MRE now includes a **complete HRW Manager** with epoch-based configuration and Redis Streams integration for high-performance transaction routing.

pages.name: mempool-routing-engine
pages.title: рҹҺҜ HRW Manager Features
pages.content:
- **Epoch-based Configuration**: Historical transaction placements remain stable while allowing dynamic weight updates
- **Redis Streams Publishing**: Real-time transaction placement decisions published to Redis Streams
- **Dynamic Weight Updates**: Automatic weight updates via `ChangeWeightage()` when new weights are calculated
- **Multi-format NodeID Support**: Flexible NodeID to ShardID conversion for various naming patterns
- **Weight Scheduler Integration**: Automatic HRW Manager updates when new weights are calculated
- **Reflection-based Integration**: Seamless HRW Manager integration without circular imports

pages.name: mempool-routing-engine
pages.title: рҹ”„ HRW Manager Workflow
pages.content:
```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Transaction Received] --> B[HRW Manager.Place]
    B --> C[Calculate HRW Score]
    C --> D[Select Primary + Replicas]
    D --> E[Publish to Redis Streams]
    E --> F[Route to Selected Mempool]
    
    G[Weight Scheduler] --> H[Calculate New Weights]
    H --> I[Call ChangeWeightage]
    I --> J[Update HRW Manager]
    J --> K[New Epoch Created]
```

pages.title: рҹҺҜ NodeID to ShardID Conversion

The system supports various NodeID formats:

- **Pure Numbers**: `"1"` вҶ’ shard ID `1`
- **Hyphen-separated**: `"mempool-1"` вҶ’ shard ID `1`
- **Embedded numbers**: `"mempool1"` вҶ’ shard ID `1`
- **Hash-based fallback**: `"custom-node-abc"` вҶ’ consistent hash-based shard ID

pages.title: GCP Deployment & vCPU Requirements
pages.content: The MRE is designed for high-performance multi-threaded operation with parallel mempool server connections. Here are the recommended GCP instance configurations:

pages.name: mempool-routing-engine
pages.title: **Multi-Threading Architecture**
pages.content:
- **15 concurrent goroutines** per request (one per mempool server)
- **Parallel I/O operations** for maximum performance
- **Thread-safe data aggregation** across multiple servers
- **~15x faster** than sequential execution

pages.name: mempool-routing-engine
pages.title: **vCPU Requirements**
pages.content:
| Mempool Count | Minimum vCPUs | Recommended vCPUs | Memory | Use Case |
|---------------|---------------|-------------------|---------|----------|
| 5 mempools    | 4 vCPUs       | 6-8 vCPUs        | 16-32 GB | Development |
| 10 mempools   | 6 vCPUs       | 8-12 vCPUs       | 32-64 GB | Production |
| **15 mempools** | **8 vCPUs**   | **12-16 vCPUs**  | **64-128 GB** | **High-traffic Production** |

pages.name: mempool-routing-engine
pages.title: **GCP Instance Recommendations**
pages.content:
**Option 1: Cost-Optimized (Development)**

pages.name: mempool-routing-engine

pages.name: node-selection

pages.content:
- вң… **VRF Buddy Selection** - Cryptographically secure, verifiable buddy selection
- вң… **gRPC Integration** - Connect to peer directory for node discovery
- вң… **Mnemonic Support** - Generate keys from BIP39 mnemonic phrases
- вң… **Production Ready** - Comprehensive error handling and validation

pages.name: node-selection
pages.title: Complete Example
pages.content:
```go
package main

import (
    "context"
    "fmt"
    "log"

    "gossipnode/AVC/NodeSelection/pkg/selection"
)

func main() {
    fmt.Println("рҹҡҖ NodeSelection Package Example")
    fmt.Println("=================================")

    // Generate keys from mnemonic
    publicKey, privateKey, err := selection.GenerateKeysFromMnemonic("abandon abandon abandon abandon abandon abandon abandon abandon abandon abandon abandon about")
    if err != nil {
        log.Fatalf("вқҢ Failed to generate keys from mnemonic: %v", err)
    }

    fmt.Printf("вң… Generated keys from mnemonic\n")
    fmt.Printf("   Public Key: %x...\n", publicKey[:8])
    fmt.Println()

    // Configuration
    nodeID := "my-node-001"
    networkSalt := []byte("my-network-salt-2024")
    peerDirAddress := "localhost:50051"
    numBuddies := 3

    fmt.Printf("рҹ”‘ Node ID: %s\n", nodeID)
    fmt.Printf("рҹ“Ў Peer Directory: %s\n", peerDirAddress)
    fmt.Printf("рҹҺІ Selecting: %d buddies\n", numBuddies)
    fmt.Println()

    // Select buddy nodes
    ctx := context.Background()
    buddies, err := selection.GetBuddyNodes(
        ctx,
        nodeID,
        privateKey,
        networkSalt,
        peerDirAddress,
        numBuddies,
    )
    if err != nil {
        log.Fatalf("вқҢ Failed to select buddies: %v", err)
    }

    // Display results
    fmt.Printf("вң… Successfully selected %d buddies!\n\n", len(buddies))
    fmt.Println("Selected Buddies:")
    fmt.Println("=================")
    for i, buddy := range buddies {
        fmt.Printf("%2d. %-20s | %s | Score: %.2f | ASN: %d\n",
            i+1,
            buddy.Node.PeerId,
            buddy.Node.Address,
            buddy.Node.SelectionScore,
            buddy.Node.ASN,
        )
    }

    fmt.Println("\nвңЁ Example completed successfully!")
}
```

pages.name: node-selection
pages.title: `GetBuddyNodes(ctx, nodeID, privateKey, networkSalt, peerDirAddress, numBuddies)`
pages.content:
Selects buddy nodes from a peer directory service.

**Parameters:**
- `ctx` - Context for cancellation/timeout
- `nodeID` - Your node's unique identifier
- `privateKey` - Ed25519 private key for VRF operations
- `networkSalt` - Network-specific salt for VRF calculations
- `peerDirAddress` - Address of peer directory service ("host:port")
- `numBuddies` - Number of buddy nodes to select

**Returns:** `([]*BuddyNode, error)`

pages.name: node-selection
pages.title: `GetBuddyNodesWithNodes(ctx, nodeID, privateKey, networkSalt, nodes, numBuddies)`
pages.content:
Selects buddy nodes from a provided list of nodes (useful for testing).

**Parameters:**
- `ctx` - Context for cancellation/timeout
- `nodeID` - Your node's unique identifier
- `privateKey` - Ed25519 private key for VRF operations
- `networkSalt` - Network-specific salt for VRF calculations
- `nodes` - Slice of available nodes
- `numBuddies` - Number of buddy nodes to select

**Returns:** `([]*BuddyNode, error)`

pages.name: node-selection
pages.title: `GenerateKeysFromMnemonic(mnemonic)`
pages.content:
Generates Ed25519 keys from a BIP39 mnemonic phrase.

**Parameters:**
- `mnemonic` - BIP39 mnemonic phrase (12 or 24 words)

**Returns:** `(ed25519.PublicKey, ed25519.PrivateKey, error)`
