pages.name: running-a-node
pages.title: Running a JMDN Node
pages.content:
id: running-a-node
title: Running a JMDN Node
sidebar_label: Running a Node
description: Complete guide to building, installing, and running a JMDT Decentralised Node (JMDN) вҖ” covering dependency setup, cross-platform binary compilation, service installation, and deployment.
keywords: [JMDN node, run JMDT node, build JMDN, JMDT validator, node setup, immudb, Yggdrasil, deploy JMDN, JMDT Layer 2 node]

pages.name: running-a-node
pages.title: Running a JMDN Node
pages.content:
> *The Truth Layer for Verifiable Information вҖ” join the JMDT network by running your own JMDN node.*

This guide covers the complete workflow for setting up a **JMDN** (JMDT Decentralised Node): installing dependencies, building the binary, installing system services, and deploying updates. All scripts are cross-platform and support Linux, macOS, and FreeBSD.

pages.name: running-a-node
pages.title: Overview
pages.content:
The JMDN setup workflow uses four scripts in the `Scripts/` directory:

| Script | Purpose |
|---|---|
| `Scripts/setup_dependencies.sh` | Install Go, immudb, Yggdrasil, and build tools |
| `Scripts/build.sh` | Compile the `jmdn` binary from source |
| `Scripts/install_services.sh` | Register `jmdn` and `immudb` as system services |
| `Scripts/deploy.sh` | Full automated build + atomic swap + service restart |
| `Scripts/start_jmdn_wrapper.sh` | Runtime wrapper invoked by the init system |

pages.name: running-a-node
pages.title: Supported Platforms
pages.content:
| Platform | Service Manager | Package Manager |
|---|---|---|
| Ubuntu / Debian | systemd | apt |
| RHEL / CentOS | systemd | dnf / yum |
| Arch Linux | systemd | pacman |
| Alpine Linux | OpenRC | apk |
| macOS | launchd | Homebrew |
| FreeBSD | rc.d | pkg |

pages.name: running-a-node
pages.title: Step 1 вҖ” Install Dependencies
pages.content:
Run the dependency installer as root. Use `--all` to install everything in one shot:

```bash
sudo ./Scripts/setup_dependencies.sh --all
```

This installs:
- **Go** (minimum version per `GO_FALLBACK_VER` in the script) вҖ” required to compile JMDN
- **immudb** вҖ” the tamper-proof immutable ledger used by JMDN for local chain storage
- **Yggdrasil** вҖ” encrypted mesh network used for alternative peer messaging
- **GCC / build-essential** вҖ” C toolchain required for CGO compilation

You can install individual components if needed:

```bash
sudo ./Scripts/setup_dependencies.sh --go
sudo ./Scripts/setup_dependencies.sh --immudb
sudo ./Scripts/setup_dependencies.sh --yggdrasil
```

> **Note:** The script detects your platform and package manager automatically. It also checks for existing installations and only upgrades if your current version is older than the target.

pages.name: running-a-node
pages.title: Step 2 вҖ” Build the Binary
pages.content:
After installing dependencies, build the `jmdn` binary:

```bash
./Scripts/build.sh
```

This will:
1. Run `go mod tidy` to clean up dependencies
2. Embed git metadata (commit, branch, tag, build time) into the binary via ldflags
3. Compile with `CGO_ENABLED=1` (required for immudb's SQLite integration)
4. Output the binary as `./jmdn` in the current directory

To build into a specific output directory:

```bash
./Scripts/build.sh /usr/local/bin
```

pages.name: running-a-node
pages.title: Build Requirements
pages.content:
- GCC must be installed (run `setup_dependencies.sh` first if unsure)
- Go must be on `PATH` вҖ” the script will remind you if it isn't

pages.name: running-a-node
pages.title: Step 3 вҖ” Install System Services
pages.content:
Install `jmdn` and `immudb` as system services so they start automatically on boot:

```bash
sudo ./Scripts/install_services.sh
```

This script:
1. Creates required directories (`data/`, `config/`, `DB/`, logs)
2. Copies the `jmdn` binary and `start_jmdn_wrapper.sh` to the system binary path
3. Installs and enables the appropriate service definitions for your platform

pages.name: running-a-node
pages.title: Service Definitions Installed
pages.content:
Two services are created:

- **`immudb`** вҖ” The immutable ledger backing JMDN's local chain state
- **`jmdn`** вҖ” The JMDT Decentralised Node (depends on immudb)

pages.name: running-a-node
pages.title: Configuration File
pages.content:
Before starting, ensure your node configuration file is in place:

```bash

pages.name: running-a-node
pages.title: Copy the default config template
pages.content: cp jmdn_default.yaml /etc/jmdn/jmdn.yaml

pages.name: running-a-node
pages.title: Edit to set your node identity, seed node address, and network parameters
pages.content:
nano /etc/jmdn/jmdn.yaml
```

> If `/etc/jmdn/jmdn.yaml` is missing, `start_jmdn_wrapper.sh` will automatically fall back to `./jmdn.yaml` in the working directory вҖ” useful for local development.

pages.name: running-a-node
pages.title: Starting Services Manually
pages.content:
After installation, start the services using the commands for your platform:

**Linux (systemd):**
```bash
sudo systemctl start immudb
sudo systemctl start jmdn

pages.name: running-a-node
pages.title: Check status
pages.content:
sudo systemctl status jmdn
```

**macOS (launchd):**
```bash
launchctl start com.jmdn.immudb
launchctl start com.jmdn.jmdn
```

**Alpine Linux (OpenRC):**
```bash
sudo rc-service immudb start
sudo rc-service jmdn start
```

**FreeBSD (rc.d):**
```bash
sudo service immudb start
sudo service jmdn start
```

pages.name: running-a-node
pages.title: Step 4 вҖ” Deploy Updates
pages.content:
To rebuild and redeploy a new version of JMDN with zero manual steps:

```bash
sudo ./Scripts/deploy.sh
```

The deploy script performs a fully automated pipeline:

1. **Dependency check** вҖ” Runs `setup_dependencies.sh --all` to ensure environment is current
2. **Build** вҖ” Calls `build.sh` to compile the new binary
3. **Atomic swap** вҖ” Backs up the current binary, then atomically replaces it
4. **Service restart** вҖ” Restarts the `jmdn` service using the platform-appropriate service manager
5. **Health check** вҖ” Polls service status up to 10 times (3-second intervals) to confirm the node is running
6. **Rollback** вҖ” If the health check fails, automatically restores the previous binary and restarts

pages.name: running-a-node
pages.title: Environment Path
pages.content:
The deploy script ensures Go is on `PATH`:

```bash
export PATH="/usr/local/go/bin:${PATH}"
```

This is set automatically вҖ” no manual profile changes needed when running via Ansible or CI.

pages.name: running-a-node
pages.title: Runtime Wrapper
pages.content:
The `start_jmdn_wrapper.sh` script is the entry point used by the init system (systemd, launchd, rc.d, OpenRC). It is self-contained by design вҖ” it does not depend on any library or external script, so it remains functional after being copied to the system binary directory at install time.

It resolves paths in the following order:

1. Environment variables (`BIN_PATH`, `CONFIG_PATH`, `JMDN_BIN`, `JMDN_ETC`)
2. Platform-appropriate defaults (e.g., `/usr/local/bin` on Linux, Homebrew prefix on macOS)
3. Local fallback: `./jmdn.yaml` in the working directory (development convenience)

Override at runtime if needed:

```bash
BIN_PATH=/custom/path/jmdn CONFIG_PATH=/custom/jmdn.yaml ./start_jmdn_wrapper.sh
```

pages.name: seednode-core
pages.title: Seed Node
pages.content:
A decentralized **peer directory service** for the JMDT network.  
The system maps **PeerIDs вҶ’ IPs/multiaddrs + metadata**, and optionally supports **human-readable names** (`alice.node.jmdt`) as aliases.

pages.name: seednode-core
pages.title: вңЁ Features
pages.content:
- **Canonical identity**:  
  Every node is anchored to its **PeerID** (Ed25519-based).
- **Alias support**:  
  Human-friendly names like `alice.node.jmdt` mapped to PeerIDs (ENS-style).
- **Peer records**:  
  Store IPv4/IPv6, multiaddrs, neighbor connections, and binary status (`active/inactive`).
- **V, R, S signature format**:  
  Uses Ethereum-compatible V, R, S signature components for cryptographic verification.
- **Auto-generated timestamps**:  
  Timestamps are automatically generated server-side for consistency.
- **Auto-active status**:  
  New peer registrations are automatically marked as active.
- **Signed updates**:  
  Only the PeerID owner can update their record.
- **Fast lookups**:  
  PostgreSQL-based storage for fast and reliable reads.
- **Durable source of truth**:  
  PostgreSQL database stores all peer records and aliases.
- **Dynamic liveness**:  
  Heartbeat messages for real-time status tracking.
- **Auditability**:  
  Complete audit trail with timestamps and signatures.
- **Binary Status System**:  
  Simple active/inactive status with clear visual indicators.
- **рҹҶ• Automatic Database Setup**:  
  Service automatically creates database and applies schema on startup.
- **рҹҶ• Health Checks**:  
  Comprehensive PostgreSQL connectivity and database validation.
- **рҹҶ• Fail-Fast Design**:  
  Service kills itself immediately if PostgreSQL is unavailable.
- **рҹҶ• IP Geolocation**:  
  Automatic region detection from IPv4/IPv6 addresses using IP-API (free) or ipapi.co (paid).
- **рҹҶ• Neighbor Management**:  
  Full CRUD operations for peer neighbor relationships with proper database normalization.
- **рҹҶ• Inactive Peer Reporting**:  
  Comprehensive system for reporting and tracking inactive peers with configurable thresholds.
- **рҹҶ• Health Check System**:  
  RPC-based health checks for individual peers with timeout and retry logic.
- **рҹҶ• Time Window Filtering**:  
  Reports filtered by configurable time windows for better accuracy and performance.
- **рҹҶ• Buddy Management**:  
  Recent buddy tracking with automatic limit management (39 records max) and smart cleanup.

- **вң… Type Safety**: Compile-time validation for all database operations
- **вң… Better Error Handling**: Clear, actionable error messages

pages.name: seednode-core

- **Type Safety**: Compile-time validation prevents runtime errors
- **Better Maintainability**: Clean, readable code with clear separation of concerns
- **Automatic Mapping**: No manual data conversion between Go structs and database rows

pages.name: seednode-core

pages.content:
The peer directory uses a **simplified binary status system**:

- **вң… Active (1)**: Peer is online and responding
- **вқҢ Inactive (0)**: Peer is offline or unresponsive

**Benefits:**
- Cleaner UI with green/red status indicators

- Better performance and reduced complexity

pages.title: вҡ–пёҸ Peer Weights System (v3.3) - **NEW FEATURE**
pages.content: The peer directory now supports **peer weights** for priority-based routing and load balancing:

pages.name: seednode-core
pages.title: **Use Cases:**
pages.content:

- **Priority Routing**: Prefer peers with higher weights for operations

  - `0.5` - Medium priority
  - `0.85` - High priority
  - `1.0` - Maximum priority

pages.name: seednode-core
pages.title: рҹ‘Ҙ Buddy Management (v4.1) - **NEW FEATURE**
pages.content: The peer directory now includes **buddy management** functionality to maintain a list of recently interacted peers:

pages.name: seednode-core
pages.title: **What's New:**
pages.content:
- **вң… Recent Buddy Tracking**: Maintains a list of recently interacted peers
- **вң… Automatic Limit Management**: Automatically maintains a maximum of 39 records
- **вң… Smart Cleanup**: Removes oldest 13 records when limit is exceeded
- **вң… Exclusion Filtering**: ListBuddy returns all peers except recent buddies
- **вң… Transaction Safety**: All operations are wrapped in database transactions
- **вң… Non-Existent Peer Handling**: Skips peers that don't exist in the system

pages.name: seednode-core
pages.title: **UpdateBuddy**
pages.content:
- Adds new peer IDs to the recent buddy list
- Automatically maintains the 39-record limit
- Updates timestamps for existing buddies
- Returns count of added and removed records

pages.name: seednode-core
pages.title: **ListBuddy**
pages.content:
- Returns all peers in the system
- **Excludes** peers that are in the recent buddy list
- Useful for finding new peers to interact with
- Maintains full peer record information

pages.name: seednode-core
pages.title: **Database Schema:**
pages.content:
```sql
-- Recent buddy table (max 39 records, oldest 13 removed when limit reached)
CREATE TABLE recent_buddy (
    id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY,
    peer_id VARCHAR(255) NOT NULL REFERENCES peer_records(peer_id) ON DELETE CASCADE,
    added_at BIGINT NOT NULL DEFAULT EXTRACT(EPOCH FROM NOW()),
    created_at TIMESTAMP WITH TIME ZONE DEFAULT NOW(),
    UNIQUE(peer_id)
);
```

pages.name: seednode-core
pages.title: **Use Cases:**
pages.content:
- **Peer Discovery**: Find new peers to connect to (exclude recent buddies)
- **Load Distribution**: Avoid repeatedly connecting to the same peers
- **Network Expansion**: Discover fresh peers for better network coverage
- **Connection Management**: Track recently used connections for optimization

pages.name: seednode-core
pages.title: **API Usage:**
pages.content: ```bash

pages.name: seednode-core
pages.title: Update recent buddy list

pages.name: seednode-core
pages.title: List peers excluding recent buddies
pages.content:
grpcurl -plaintext -d '{}' localhost:17002 peerpb.PeerDirectory/ListBuddy
```

pages.title: **UpdateBuddy Response:**

```json
{

}
```

pages.name: seednode-core
pages.title: **ListBuddy Response:**

pages.name: seednode-core
pages.title: **Automatic Limit Management:**
pages.content:
- **Maximum Records**: 39 peers in recent buddy list
- **Cleanup Trigger**: Every UpdateBuddy call
- **Cleanup Amount**: Removes oldest 13 records when limit exceeded
- **Database Function**: `maintain_recent_buddy_limit()` handles cleanup automatically

pages.name: seednode-core
pages.title: Database Layer Structure
pages.content:
The system now uses a clean GORM-based architecture:

```
pkg/peer/
в”ңв”Җв”Җ models.go              # GORM models with proper tags
в”ңв”Җв”Җ gorm_repository.go     # GORM repository implementation
в”ңв”Җв”Җ gorm_jmns_service.go   # GORM-integrated service layer
в”ңв”Җв”Җ gorm_db.go            # Database connection utilities
в”ңв”Җв”Җ types.go              # Internal type definitions
в”ңв”Җв”Җ geolocation.go        # Basic geolocation service with lookup tables
в”ңв”Җв”Җ geolocation_api.go    # HTTP API services (IP-API, ipapi.co, IPStack)
в”ңв”Җв”Җ geolocation_config.go # Configuration management for geolocation
в””в”Җв”Җ geolocation_test.go   # Unit tests for geolocation functionality
```

pages.name: seednode-core
pages.title: PeerRecord
pages.content:
```go
type PeerRecord struct {
    PeerID        string         `gorm:"primaryKey;column:peer_id;type:varchar(255)"`
    Multiaddrs    pq.StringArray `gorm:"type:text[];not null"`
    Seq           uint64         `gorm:"not null;default:1"`
    FirstCreated  int64          `gorm:"not null"`
    LastUpdated   int64          `gorm:"not null"`
    ActiveFrom    int64          `gorm:"not null"`
    CurrentStatus int            `gorm:"not null;default:1"`
    Neighbors     pq.StringArray `gorm:"type:text[];default:'{}'"`
    Labels        datatypes.JSON `gorm:"type:jsonb;default:'{}'"`
    Weights       float64        `gorm:"type:real;default:0.0"`
    V             string         `gorm:"not null"`
    R             string         `gorm:"not null"`
    S             string         `gorm:"not null"`
    CreatedAt     time.Time      `gorm:"autoCreateTime"`
    UpdatedAt     time.Time      `gorm:"autoUpdateTime"`
}
```

pages.name: seednode-core
pages.title: GormPeerAlias
pages.content:
```go
type GormPeerAlias struct {
    Name        string    `gorm:"primaryKey;column:name;type:varchar(255)"`
    PeerID      string    `gorm:"not null;column:peer_id;type:varchar(255)"`
    Created     int64     `gorm:"not null"`
    LastUpdated int64     `gorm:"not null"`
    V           string    `gorm:"not null"`
    R           string    `gorm:"not null"`
    S           string    `gorm:"not null"`
    CreatedAt   time.Time `gorm:"autoCreateTime"`
    UpdatedAt   time.Time `gorm:"autoUpdateTime"`
}
```

pages.name: seednode-core
pages.title: Heartbeat
pages.content:
```go
type Heartbeat struct {
    ID         uint64         `gorm:"primaryKey;autoIncrement"`
    PeerID     string         `gorm:"not null;column:peer_id;type:varchar(255)"`
    Timestamp  int64          `gorm:"not null"`
    Status     int64          `gorm:"not null"`
    Multiaddrs pq.StringArray `gorm:"type:text[]"`
    V          string         `gorm:"not null"`
    R          string         `gorm:"not null"`
    S          string         `gorm:"not null"`
    CreatedAt  time.Time      `gorm:"autoCreateTime"`
}
```

pages.name: seednode-core
pages.title: Key GORM Features
pages.content:
- **AutoMigrate**: Automatic schema creation and updates
- **Type Safety**: Compile-time validation for all operations
- **JSON Support**: Native JSONB handling with `datatypes.JSON`
- **Array Support**: PostgreSQL arrays with `pq.StringArray`
- **Timestamps**: Automatic `CreatedAt` and `UpdatedAt` management
- **Foreign Keys**: Proper relationship constraints
- **Connection Pooling**: Efficient database connection management

pages.name: seednode-core
pages.title: Database Schema
pages.content: The system uses PostgreSQL with the following key tables:

pages.name: seednode-core
pages.title: peer_records
pages.content:
```sql
CREATE TABLE peer_records (
    peer_id VARCHAR(255) PRIMARY KEY,
    multiaddrs TEXT[] NOT NULL,
    seq BIGINT NOT NULL DEFAULT 1,
    first_created BIGINT NOT NULL,
    last_updated BIGINT NOT NULL,
    active_from BIGINT NOT NULL,
    current_status INTEGER NOT NULL DEFAULT 1,
    neighbors TEXT[] DEFAULT '{}',
    labels JSONB DEFAULT '{}',
    weights REAL DEFAULT 0.0,           -- NEW: Peer weight field
    v TEXT NOT NULL,
    r TEXT NOT NULL,
    s TEXT NOT NULL,
    created_at TIMESTAMP WITH TIME ZONE DEFAULT NOW(),
    updated_at TIMESTAMP WITH TIME ZONE DEFAULT NOW()
);
```

**Key Fields**:
- `weights`: Float value (0.0-1.0) representing peer priority/weight
- `labels`: JSONB field for metadata (region, role, etc.)
- `neighbors`: Array of connected peer IDs

| `GetPeer` | Get peer by ID | `GetPeerRequest` | `GetPeerResponse` |
| `DeletePeer` | Delete peer | `DeletePeerRequest` | `DeletePeerResponse` |
| `ListPeers` | List peers with filtering | `PeerListRequest` | `PeerListResponse` |

pages.name: seednode-core
pages.title: Status Values
pages.content:
The system uses a **binary status system**:
- `PEER_STATUS_ACTIVE` (1) - Peer is online and active
- `PEER_STATUS_INACTIVE` (0) - Peer is offline or inactive

pages.name: seednode-core
pages.title: Weights System
pages.content:
The **weights field** allows peers to be assigned numerical weights for various purposes:
- **Load Balancing**: Higher weights can indicate more capable peers
- **Priority Routing**: Peers with higher weights may be preferred for certain operations
- **Resource Allocation**: Weights can represent computational power, bandwidth, or reliability
- **Consensus Participation**: Weights can influence voting power in consensus mechanisms

**Weight Values**:
- Range: `0.0` to `1.0` (recommended)
- Default: `0.0` (no special weight)
- Type: `float64` in Go, `float` in protobuf, `REAL` in PostgreSQL

**Usage Examples**:
- `0.0` - Standard peer with no special weight
- `0.5` - Medium priority peer
- `0.85` - High priority peer
- `1.0` - Maximum priority peer

pages.name: seednode
pages.title: Overview
pages.content: The SeedNode module provides peer discovery and registration services for the JMZK network. It enables nodes to register with seed nodes, discover neighbors, and manage peer relationships.

pages.name: seednode
pages.title: Purpose
pages.content:
The SeedNode module enables:
- Peer registration with seed nodes
- Peer discovery and neighbor management
- Public IP detection
- Peer alias management
- Heartbeat monitoring
- VRS (V, R, S) signature generation for peer records

pages.name: seednode
pages.title: 1. SeedNode Client
pages.content:
**File:** `seednode.go`

Client for communicating with seed nodes:
- `NewClient`: Create new seed node client
- `RegisterPeer`: Register peer with seed node
- `RegisterPeerWithAlias`: Register peer with alias
- `SendHeartbeat`: Send heartbeat to seed node
- `DiscoverAndAddNeighbors`: Discover and add neighbors
- `GetNeighbors`: Get neighbor list
- `AddNeighbor`: Add neighbor relationship

pages.name: seednode
pages.title: 2. Signature Generation
pages.content:
**File:** `signature.go`

VRS signature generation for peer records:
- `SignPeerRecord`: Sign peer record with VRS signature
- `SignAlias`: Sign alias with VRS signature
- `SignHeartbeat`: Sign heartbeat with VRS signature

pages.name: seednode
pages.title: 3. Protocol Buffers
pages.content:
**File:** `proto/seednode.proto`

gRPC service definitions:
- `PeerDirectory`: Main service interface
- `RegisterPeer`: Register peer request/response
- `RegisterPeerWithAlias`: Register peer with alias
- `SendHeartbeat`: Heartbeat request/response
- `GetNeighbors`: Get neighbors request/response
- `AddNeighbor`: Add neighbor request/response

id: sequencer
title: Sequencer Module
sidebar_label: Sequencer
description: JMDT's Sequencer module вҖ” responsible for consensus orchestration, buddy node selection, vote collection, BFT consensus execution, and transaction ordering on the JMDT Layer 2 network.
keywords: [JMDT sequencer, transaction ordering, consensus orchestration, BFT, Layer 2 sequencer, buddy nodes, JMDN]

pages.name: sequencer
pages.title: Sequencer Module
pages.content:
> *The Truth Layer for Verifiable Information вҖ” transaction ordering and consensus orchestration on JMDT L2.*

The **Sequencer** is the orchestration core of the JMDT Layer 2 chain. It drives the AVC consensus cycle: selecting buddy node committees via VRF, creating PubSub channels, collecting BuddyVotes, executing BFT consensus, and batching DAG state transitions into zk-proofs for L1 anchoring.

The Sequencer runs as a core component inside the **JMDN** (JMDT Decentralised Node) binary.

pages.name: sequencer
pages.title: Overview
pages.content:
The Sequencer module enables:

- Consensus orchestration for block validation
- Buddy node selection and management using VRF
- Vote collection from buddy nodes
- BFT consensus execution
- PubSub channel management for consensus
- CRDT synchronisation for buddy node state

pages.name: sequencer
pages.title: 1. Consensus
pages.content:
**File:** `JMDN/Sequencer/Consensus.go`

Main consensus orchestration:

- `NewConsensus` вҖ” Create a new consensus instance
- `Start` вҖ” Begin the consensus process for a block
- `QueryBuddyNodes` вҖ” Select buddy nodes via VRF for the current round
- `RequestSubscriptionPermission` вҖ” Request subscription from selected buddy nodes
- `CreatePubSubChannel` вҖ” Create the private PubSub channel for consensus

pages.name: sequencer
pages.title: 2. Communication
pages.content:
**File:** `JMDN/Sequencer/Communication.go`

Communication with buddy nodes:

- `AskForSubscription` вҖ” Ask buddy nodes to subscribe to the consensus channel
- `RequestVoteResultsFromBuddies` вҖ” Collect vote results from the buddy committee
- `StartBFTConsensus` вҖ” Trigger the BFT consensus phase

pages.name: sequencer
pages.title: 3. Router
pages.content:
**File:** `JMDN/Sequencer/Router/Router.go`

Routing for consensus operations:

- Message routing across JMDN peers
- Vote aggregation and tallying
- Response handling

pages.name: sequencer
pages.title: 4. Metadata
pages.content:
**File:** `JMDN/Sequencer/Metadata/Metadata.go`

Consensus metadata management:

- Block metadata
- Vote metadata
- Consensus state tracking

pages.name: sequencer
pages.title: 5. Triggers
pages.content:
**File:** `JMDN/Sequencer/Triggers/`

Consensus triggers:

- `Triggers.go` вҖ” Trigger management for consensus phases
- `Maps.go` вҖ” Vote result maps

pages.content:
id: tokenomics
title: Tokenomics & Incentives
sidebar_label: Tokenomics
description: JMDT token economics вҖ” 1 Billion hard-capped supply, token distribution across L3 development, R&D, team, and founders, vesting schedule, and utility within the JupiterMeta ecosystem.
keywords: [JMDT tokenomics, JMDT token supply, token distribution, staking, blockchain economics, 1 billion JMDT, vesting, DAO governance]

pages.name: tokenomics
pages.title: Tokenomics & Incentives
pages.content:
> The total supply of JMDT is hard capped at **1 Billion tokens**, ensuring a fixed and transparent monetary base for sustainable growth and decentralised governance.

The allocation is thoughtfully structured to incentivise participation, support long-term ecosystem development, and provide liquidity for market access.

pages.name: tokenomics
pages.title: Token Overview
pages.content:
| Property | Value |
|---|---|
| **Token Name** | JMDT (Jupiter Meta Data Token) |
| **Total Supply** | 1,000,000,000 (1 Billion) вҖ” hard cap |
| **Token Type** | Utility + Governance |
| **Primary Use** | DAG rollups, L2 commitments, node staking, DAO governance |

pages.name: tokenomics
pages.title: Token Utility
pages.content:
JMDT powers every layer of the protocol:

- **DAG Rollups** вҖ” JMDT tokens are used to commit DAG state transitions to L2
- **L2 Commitments** вҖ” Fuel Sequencer-submitted zkRollup batches to Ethereum
- **Node Staking** вҖ” Validators and Seed Nodes stake JMDT to participate in AVC consensus and earn rewards
- **DAO Governance** вҖ” Token holders vote on protocol upgrades, parameter changes, and ecosystem grants
- **Enterprise Access** вҖ” Enterprises receive JMDT via private allocation and grants to operate L3 DAG nodes

pages.name: tokenomics
pages.title: Distribution Breakdown
pages.content:
| Pool | Allocation | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **L3 Developments, Enterprises & Early Adopters** | 55% | Incentivise L3 applications, enterprise integrations, DAG-based data contributors, testnet validators, and ZK-proof participants. Anchors network utility and network effects. |
| **Founders and Promoters** | 25% | Designated for the original visionaries and promoters behind the JMDT protocol. Locked and vested to reflect long-term alignment with the ecosystem's mission. |
| **Founding Team and Employees** | 10% | Allocated to the core team responsible for engineering, cryptography, business, and operational execution. Subject to long-term vesting schedules. |
| **R&D and Foundation Initiatives** | 10% | Reserved for protocol-level research, zero-knowledge innovation, cryptographic upgrades, and grants to academic or research institutions building on JupiterMeta. |

pages.name: tokenomics
pages.title: Vesting & Unlock Strategy
pages.content:
To protect long-term network integrity and reduce sell pressure:

- All team, promoter, and investor tokens are subject to **cliff-based linear vesting**
- Early adopter tokens are released in **phased batches** over a 6вҖ“12 month horizon
- The Foundation's deployment of ecosystem grants and treasury tokens is governed by internal frameworks

pages.name: tokenomics
pages.title: Incentive Design
pages.content:
This allocation model ensures the **JMDT token remains utility-focused**, incentivises genuine contribution, and unlocks sustainable value for all stakeholders:

- **Developers** вҖ” grants and ecosystem rewards for building on JMDT
- **Data contributors** вҖ” rewards for participating in the ZK-verified data economy via SuperJ and enterprise DAGs
- **Validators** вҖ” staking rewards for honest AVC consensus participation
- **Enterprise partners** вҖ” private allocations for deploying L3 DAG infrastructure

The largest pool (55%) directly supports **real-world adoption** вҖ” the greatest signal of long-term protocol health.

pages.title: Use Cases
pages.content:
id: use-cases
title: Use Cases
sidebar_label: Use Cases
description: Real-world use cases for JMDT вҖ” from consumer identity and reward monetisation via SuperJ, to enterprise data collaboration, AI-driven analytics, DeFi, and compliance-first applications.
keywords: [JMDT use cases, SuperJ, Hercules, blockchain identity, enterprise DAG, DeFi privacy, Layer 2 applications, Web3 data marketplace]

pages.name: use-cases
pages.title: Use Cases
pages.content: > **JMDT вҖ” The Truth Layer for Verifiable Information.** Every use case is grounded in privacy-first, cryptographically verifiable data flows вҖ” from individual users to enterprise ecosystems.

pages.name: use-cases
pages.title: SuperJ вҖ” Consumer Intelligence & Reward Platform
pages.content:
**SuperJ** is a decentralised consumer intelligence app built on JMDT's L3 DAG infrastructure. It currently enables **over 20 million users** to:

- ZK-verify their identity without exposing personal information
- Share data and provide verified insights to enterprises
- Monetise their own authentic data вҖ” earning JMDT tokens as rewards

SuperJ demonstrates JMDT's core value proposition: users own their data, enterprises access verified intelligence with a cryptographic chain of custody, and no PII is ever exposed on-chain.

**How it works:**
1. User joins SuperJ and receives a **JMDT DID** (Decentralised Identifier)
2. Engages in surveys, tasks, or enterprise data-sharing workflows
3. Earns **JMDT tokens** вҖ” redeemable for gift cards, assets, or ecosystem benefits
4. Data is shared via ZK-proofs вҖ” enterprises receive verified signals, not raw PII

pages.name: use-cases
pages.title: Hercules вҖ” Research & Decision Intelligence
pages.content:
**Hercules** is a research assistant for data-backed decision making, built on JMDT's verifiable data infrastructure. Enterprises use Hercules to:

- Query verified, anonymised datasets from the JMDT ecosystem
- Generate AI-driven insights with a cryptographic chain of custody
- Comply with GDPR and data sovereignty regulations by design

pages.name: use-cases
pages.title: Enterprise Identity Verification
pages.content:
1. Businesses onboard users via **JMDT DID credentials**
2. DID credentials are stored on **Layer 2** вҖ” verifiable but private
3. Users can share credentials across **multiple platforms** without re-exposing identity
4. Enterprise-managed DIDs enable department- or role-level access control

pages.name: use-cases
pages.title: Private Enterprise Data Operations (L3 DAG)
pages.content:
Enterprises deploy private **DAG nodes** on JMDT's L3 for:

- **Supply chain tracking** вҖ” tamper-proof audit trails via immudb
- **Compliance logging** вҖ” append-only, ZK-anchored activity records
- **CRM and analytics** вҖ” AI-inference logs with verifiable provenance
- **Internal tokenisation** вҖ” industry-specific logic and reward mechanisms

All DAG activity is periodically committed to L2 as a zk-proof, providing global auditability without exposing raw enterprise data.

pages.name: use-cases
pages.title: Cross-Enterprise Data Collaboration
pages.content:
Using the **InterDAG Bridge**, organisations can:

- Collaborate on shared datasets without centralising control
- Enable access requests, logging, and secure off-chain queries
- Maintain data sovereignty while participating in shared ecosystems

pages.name: use-cases
pages.title: Privacy-Preserving DeFi
pages.content:
- Deploy Solidity smart contracts on JMDT's EVM-compatible L2
- Benefit from ZKP-backed transaction privacy вҖ” balances and counterparties are not exposed
- Lower gas costs via zkRollup batching to Ethereum L1

pages.name: use-cases
pages.title: Web3 Data Marketplaces
pages.content:
- Build marketplaces where verified human data is tokenised and traded
- DID-based access control ensures only authorised parties can query datasets
- zk-proofed data capture enables GDPR-compliant, monetisable data flows

pages.name: use-cases
pages.title: AI-Driven Analytics Pipelines
pages.content:
- Build AI inference pipelines on L3 DAG nodes
- Outputs are cryptographically committed to L2 вҖ” audit-ready by design
- Combine human-verified identity signals with enterprise data for trusted AI

pages.name: use-cases
pages.title: How Enterprises Build on JMDT
pages.content:
| Step | Action |
|---|---|
| **1. Choose Your Layer** | Deploy an L3 DAG node for enterprise data, or build directly on L2 |
| **2. Set Up DID & Access** | Register a DID, manage keys with secure wallets, issue sub-identities |
| **3. Deploy Workflows** | Build DAG-based business logic in Python or Rust |
| **4. Rollup to L2** | Batch DAG transactions into zk-SNARK proofs, submit to JMDT L2 |
| **5. Use JMDT Token** | Fuel DAG rollups, L2 commitments, and node staking |
| **6. Run Infrastructure** | Host on GCP, AWS, or on-prem with JMDT.io RPC and immudb-based logs |
| **7. Monitor & Reward** | Use Explorer dashboards to visualise DAG graphs, rollup health, and proof verification |

pages.name: zk
pages.title: Zero-Knowledge Proofs & RISC Zero zkVM
pages.content:
id: zk
title: Zero-Knowledge Proofs & RISC Zero zkVM
sidebar_label: Zero-Knowledge Proofs
description: How JMDT uses Zero-Knowledge Proofs (zk-SNARKs and zk-STARKs) via the RISC Zero zkVM and Rust-based circuits to batch transactions privately, verify state transitions, and commit succinct proofs to Ethereum.
keywords: [Zero-Knowledge Proofs, ZKP, zk-SNARKs, zk-STARKs, RISC Zero zkVM, JMDT ZK, private transactions, Ethereum ZK, L2 ZK proofs, Rust ZK circuits]

pages.name: zk
pages.title: Zero-Knowledge Proofs & RISC Zero zkVM
pages.content:
> **The Truth Layer for Verifiable Information.** JMDT uses Zero-Knowledge Proofs as the cryptographic backbone for privacy-preserving identity, private transactions, and verifiable state transitions вҖ” without exposing any underlying data.

Zero-Knowledge Proofs (ZKPs) allow one party to prove knowledge of certain information without revealing the information itself. In JMDT, ZKPs ensure **privacy and security while maintaining verifiability** across financial, identity, and enterprise applications.

pages.name: zk
pages.title: Two Types of ZK Proofs
pages.content: JMDT supports both primary ZKP variants, leveraging the **RISC Zero zkVM** for unified execution:

pages.name: zk
pages.title: zk-SNARKs (Succinct Non-Interactive Argument of Knowledge)
pages.content:
- Provide **short, efficient proofs** with minimal computational overhead
- Widely used in privacy-preserving transactions and verifiable computations
- Require a **trusted setup** (managed securely within JMDT's protocol)
- Used for: transaction-level validation, DID authentication, L2 state checks

pages.name: zk
pages.title: zk-STARKs (Scalable Transparent Argument of Knowledge)
pages.content:
- Offer **enhanced scalability and transparency** with no trusted setup required
- Use hash-based cryptography вҖ” **quantum-resistant** and highly secure
- Particularly useful for large-scale computations in a trustless, decentralised manner
- Used for: L2 вҶ’ L1 aggregation proofs, DAG state transition commitments, Ethereum finality

By integrating both SNARKs and STARKs, JMDT achieves a balance of **efficiency, security, and scalability** вҖ” eliminating trust dependencies while preserving computational performance.

pages.name: zk
pages.title: RISC Zero zkVM
pages.content: JMDT employs a dual-layer ZK architecture, leveraging custom zk-circuits written in **Rust** and executed within the **RISC Zero Zero-Knowledge Virtual Machine (zkVM)** for high-assurance proofs.

pages.name: zk
pages.title: Why RISC Zero?
pages.content:
The RISC Zero zkVM acts as a **zk-powered virtual CPU** that proves the execution of arbitrary Rust code:

- No need to define manual R1CS or arithmetic circuits
- Enables fast iteration, modular upgrade paths, and readable cryptographic logic
- Guarantees integrity and reproducibility of enterprise workflows and DAG transitions
- Full **type safety and memory safety** via Rust; circuits are easy to audit and version-control

pages.name: zk
pages.title: Workflow
pages.content:
```mermaid
graph LR
    A["Rust ZK Circuit (guest program)"] -->|"Compiled by RISC Zero toolchain"| B["zkVM Guest Binary"]
    B -->|"DAG state updates & rollup transitions executed"| C["STARK Proof + Journal (public output)"]
    C -->|"Submitted to Ethereum"| D["JMDT Smart Contract on L1"]
    D -->|"Verified on-chain"| E["L1 Finality"]
```

pages.name: zk
pages.title: 1. Transaction Validation Circuits (L2)
pages.content:
- Written in Rust as deterministic logic
- Validate balances, DID authentication, signature correctness, and state transitions within L2
- Output a **commitment hash** consumed by the zk-rollup aggregator

pages.name: zk
pages.title: 2. Aggregation & State Transition Circuits (L2 вҶ’ L1)
pages.content:
- Aggregate multiple rollup blocks or DAG state transitions
- Execute recursive verification logic or Merkle root reconciliation
- Generate a succinct **STARK proof** using RISC Zero's zkVM for Ethereum submission

pages.name: zk
pages.title: ZK in Practice: Privacy-Preserving Queries
pages.content:
JMDT introduces a **DID-based querying mechanism** that enables privacy-preserving data access:

1. Users and enterprises can query blockchain data **without exposing sensitive details**
2. **Zero-Knowledge cryptography** prevents unauthorised access while allowing verifiable computations
3. **Federated and differential privacy techniques** ensure compliance with GDPR, HIPAA, and data sovereignty regulations
4. On-device ZK computation ensures personal data is verified without ever being shared

pages.name: zk
pages.title: Security Properties
pages.content:
| Property | Guarantee |
|---|---|
| **Completeness** | An honest prover can always convince the verifier |
| **Soundness** | A malicious prover cannot forge a valid proof |
| **Zero-Knowledge** | The verifier learns nothing beyond the truth of the statement |
| **Quantum Resistance** | zk-STARK proofs use hash-based cryptography вҖ” no elliptic curve assumptions |
| **Reproducibility** | RISC Zero zkVM ensures deterministic proof generation across all nodes |

pages.name: zk
pages.title: Integration Points
pages.content:
- **AVC Consensus** вҖ” Every block proposed by the Sequencer must include a valid zk-proof; all JMDN buddy nodes verify it independently. See [AVC Consensus вҶ’](/docs/bft)
- **DID Engine** вҖ” ZKPs authenticate user identity without exposing PII. See [Decentralised Identity вҶ’](/docs/did)
- **Sequencer & MemPool** вҖ” Aggregates DAG state and triggers zkVM proof generation. See [Sequencer вҶ’](/docs/sequencer)
- **L1 Commitment** вҖ” STARK proofs submitted to the JMDT Ethereum smart contract for on-chain verification
